title.zh: 卷五十九 張衡列傳
title.raw: 卷五十九 張衡列傳 Volume 59: Biography of Zhang Heng

sentences.zh: 張衡字平子，南陽西鄂人也。

translations.literal: Zhang Heng, courtesy name Pingzi, was a man of Xi'e in Nanyang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng, whose courtesy name was Pingzi, came from Xi'e in Nanyang commandery.

translations.literal: [1] For generations they had been a prominent surname.
translations.idiomatic: [1] For generations his family had ranked among the region's most eminent lineages.

translations.literal: His grandfather Zhang Kan was Administrator of Shu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: His grandfather, Zhang Kan, had served as Administrator of Shu commandery.

sentences.zh: 衡少善屬文，游於三輔，因入京師，觀太學，遂通五經，貫六蓺。

translations.literal: In youth Heng was skilled at composing texts; he traveled through the Three Assistants regions, then entered the capital, inspected the Imperial Academy, and thereby mastered the Five Classics and threaded through the six arts.
translations.idiomatic: From an early age Zhang Heng excelled at literary composition. He journeyed through the Three Assistants circuit, continued on into the capital, and attended the Imperial Academy, until he had mastered the Five Classics and gained a thorough command of the six classical arts.

sentences.zh: 雖才高於世，而無驕尚之情。

translations.literal: Although his talent was higher than that of the age, he had no disposition of pride or self-importance.
translations.idiomatic: Though his gifts outshone those of his contemporaries, he showed none of the arrogance such gifts often breed.

sentences.zh: 常從容淡靜，不好交接俗人。

translations.literal: He was constantly calm, leisurely, placid, and quiet, and did not like to associate or mingle with vulgar people.
translations.idiomatic: He carried himself with unruffled calm and reserved detachment, and he avoided the company of shallow, worldly men.

sentences.zh: 永元中，舉孝廉不行，連辟公府不就。

translations.literal: In the Yongyuan era he was recommended as Filial and Incorrupt but did not take up office; he was repeatedly summoned to posts in the high ministers' offices but did not go.
translations.idiomatic: During the Yongyuan years he was nominated as Filial and Incorrupt yet declined to serve, and when high offices repeatedly called him to their bureaus, he still refused to answer.

sentences.zh: 時天下承平日久，自王侯以下，莫不踰侈。

translations.literal: At that time the realm had long enjoyed peace; from kings and marquises downward, none did not exceed proper limits in extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: The empire had known peace so long that, from the princes of blood down to the lowest nobles, nearly everyone indulged in wasteful excess.

sentences.zh: 衡乃擬班固《兩都》，作《二京賦》，因以諷諫。

translations.literal: Heng then modeled himself on Ban Gu's "Rhapsodies on the Two Capitals" and composed the "Rhapsodies on the Two Metropolises," using them thereby to remonstrate by indirection.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng took Ban Gu's paired rhapsodies on the two capitals as his model and wrote the "Two Metropolises," using that grand literary form to offer oblique criticism of the age.

translations.literal: He refined his thought and wove connections; only after ten years was it completed.
translations.idiomatic: He polished every line and fitted every allusion until, a full decade later, the work stood complete.

translations.literal: The text is long, therefore it is not recorded here.
translations.idiomatic: Because the piece runs to such length, the text is not reproduced in this biography.

sentences.zh: 大將軍鄧騭奇其才，累召不應。

translations.literal: Grand General Deng Zhi marveled at his talent and repeatedly summoned him, but he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand General Deng Zhi, struck by Zhang Heng's brilliance, summoned him again and again, yet Zhang Heng never answered the call.

sentences.zh: 衡善機巧，尤致思於天文﹑陰陽﹑歷筭。

translations.literal: Heng was skilled in ingenious devices and especially applied his thought to astronomy, yin-yang theory, and calendrical computation.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng had a gift for mechanical ingenuity, and he devoted his deepest attention to astronomy, cosmology, and the mathematics of the calendar.

sentences.zh: 常耽好玄經，[2]謂崔瑗曰：「吾觀太玄，方知子雲妙極道數，乃與五經相擬，非徒傳記之屬，使人難論陰陽之事，漢家得天下二百歲之書也。

translations.literal: He constantly delighted in the esoteric classic, [2] and said to Cui Yuan: "When I examined the Taixuan, I then knew that Ziyun had plumbed to the utmost the numbers of the Way; it can be set beside the Five Classics; it is not merely the sort of thing found in chronicles; it makes it hard for people to debate matters of yin and yang; it is a book for the Han house at the two-hundred-year mark of obtaining the realm."
translations.idiomatic: He was often absorbed in Yang Xiong's esoteric classic, the Taixuan, [2] and once remarked to Cui Yuan: "Only after I studied the Taixuan did I grasp how deeply Yang Ziyun had probed the numerology of the Way. The work belongs in the company of the Five Classics, not among mere narrative histories. It unsettles glib talk about yin and yang and reads like a book written for the Han at the moment it had held the realm for two hundred years."

translations.literal: [3] After another two hundred years, will it perhaps come to an end?
translations.idiomatic: [3] If another two centuries pass, will that cycle then be nearing its close?

sentences.zh: [4]所以作者之數，必顯一世，常然之符也。

translations.literal: [4] Therefore the reckoning of those who compose must manifest in one generation; it is the sign of what is constant.
translations.idiomatic: [4] That is why the fate of a writer's work is bound to declare itself within a single age; it is the token of a pattern that holds true.

translations.literal: When the Han reaches four hundred years, the Xuan will rise.
translations.idiomatic: When the Han has ruled for four hundred years, the Taixuan teaching will flourish.

sentences.zh: 」[5]安帝雅聞衡善術學，公車特徵拜郎中，再遷為太史令。

translations.literal: " [5] Emperor An had long heard that Heng was skilled in technical learning; the imperial coaches specially summoned him and appointed him Gentleman of the Household; he was twice promoted and became Prefect Grand Astrologer.
translations.idiomatic: " [5] Emperor An had long heard of Zhang Heng's mastery of the technical sciences. The court summoned him by special edict through the coach service, appointed him a Gentleman of the Household, and twice promoted him until he reached the post of Prefect Grand Astrologer.

sentences.zh: [6]遂乃研核陰陽，妙盡琁機之正，作渾天儀，著靈憲﹑筭罔論，言甚詳明。

translations.literal: [6] Thereupon he investigated and verified yin and yang, subtly exhausting the correctness of the turning mechanism; he made an armillary sphere and composed the Lingxian and the Suanshu Lun, his statements being very detailed and clear.
translations.idiomatic: [6] He then pressed his inquiry into yin and yang until he had mastered the true working of the celestial sphere, built a bronze armillary instrument, and wrote the Lingxian and the treatise On the Net of Computation, both works remarkable for their clarity and precision.

translations.literal: [7] (End of quotation; no additional text in the source sentence.)
translations.idiomatic: [7] (The source line marks a commentary note with no narrative text.)

sentences.zh: 順帝初，再轉，復為太史令。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Shun's reign, after further transfers, he again became Prefect Grand Astrologer.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Shun came to the throne, Zhang Heng was rotated through other posts and then returned once more to the office of Prefect Grand Astrologer.

sentences.zh: 衡不慕當世，所居之官，輒積年不徙。

translations.literal: Heng did not admire the present age; in the offices he occupied, he often went for accumulated years without transfer.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng had no appetite for the fashions of the court; whatever title he held, he often kept it for years without advancement.

sentences.zh: 自去史職，五載復還，乃設客問，作應閒以見其志云：[8]

translations.literal: From the time he left the historian's office, after five years he returned again; thereupon he set up a guest's question and composed the Response to Idle Criticism in order to manifest his intentions, editorial mark [8] saying:
translations.idiomatic: [8] Five years after leaving the astrologer's bureau he was sent back to it; he then framed a visitor's questions and answered them in his Response to Idle Criticism to set out what he truly believed.

sentences.zh: 有閒餘者曰：蓋聞前哲首務，務於下學上達，佐國理民，有雲為也。

translations.literal: There was one at leisure who said: I have heard that the foremost task of former worthies was to devote effort to lower learning and upper reaching, to assist the state and order the people; there is something to be said and done.
translations.idiomatic: A critic with time on his hands declared: "The sages of old are said to have begun with humble study and risen to high principle—to help govern the realm and bring order to the people. Surely that means more than empty words; it demands real deeds."

translations.literal: [9] What they heard in the morning, they carried out in the evening.
translations.idiomatic: [9] They would hear a lesson at dawn and put it into practice before nightfall.

translations.literal: To establish merit and establish affairs is thereby to display virtuous repute.
translations.idiomatic: Build lasting achievement, and your good name will ring out like a clear bell.

sentences.zh: [10]是故伊尹思使君為堯舜，而民處唐虞，彼豈虛言而已哉，必旌厥素爾。

translations.literal: [10] Therefore Yi Yin thought to make his lord into Yao and Shun, and the people to dwell in the age of Tang and Yu; were those mere empty words? Surely he displayed their plain substance.
translations.idiomatic: [10] That is why Yi Yin labored to make his prince another Yao or Shun and the common folk citizens of the age of Tang and Yu—far more than rhetoric: he was giving full play to what they truly were.

sentences.zh: [11]咎單、巫咸，寔守王家，[12]申伯、樊仲，實干周邦，服袞而朝，介圭作瑞。

translations.literal: [11] Gao Dan and Wu Xian truly guarded the royal house; [12] the Earl of Shen and Fan Zhong truly served the Zhou state; wearing ceremonial robes they attended court, and their great jade tablets served as auspicious tokens.
translations.idiomatic: [11] Gao Dan and Wu Xian stood as true bulwarks of the royal house; [12] the Earl of Shen and Fan Zhong were pillars of the Zhou domain—robed for court, their tall jade sceptres the emblems of their service.

sentences.zh: [13]厥跡不朽，垂烈後昆，不亦丕歟！

translations.literal: [13] Their tracks are imperishable; they let fall blazing merit to later generations—is this not great!
translations.idiomatic: [13] Their footprints cannot fade, and the fire of their deeds still warms later ages—could any praise be greater?

sentences.zh: 且學非以要利，而富貴萃之。

translations.literal: Moreover learning is not in order to demand profit, yet wealth and honor gather to it.
translations.idiomatic: Study is not undertaken merely to chase profit, yet rank and riches naturally cluster around the learned.

sentences.zh: 貴以行令，富以施惠，惠施令行，故易稱以「大業」。

translations.literal: Nobility is by issuing commands; wealth is by dispensing kindness; when kindness is dispensed and commands are carried out, therefore the Changes praises this as "great achievement."
translations.idiomatic: High station lets a man give orders; wealth lets him show generosity; when bounty flows and commands win obedience, the Book of Changes calls that "the great enterprise."

sentences.zh: [14]質以文美，實由華興，器賴雕飾為好，人以輿服為榮。

translations.literal: [14] Substance is beautified by pattern; fruit arises from blossom; vessels rely on carving and inlay to be fine; men rely on carriages and robes for honor.
translations.idiomatic: [14] Plain substance gains luster from ornament, as fruit needs the flowering branch; fine ware wants chasing and inlay, just as men look to chariots and robes for dignity.

sentences.zh: 吾子性德體道，篤信安仁，約己博蓺，無堅不鑽，以思世路，斯何遠矣！

translations.literal: "You, sir, embody virtue and pattern yourself on the Way, are steeped in good faith and rest in humaneness, restrain yourself and broaden your arts, bore through nothing that resists—in pondering the avenues of the age, how far you already are!"
translations.idiomatic: "You embody the virtues, take the Way as your frame, hold fast to trust and dwell in humaneness, discipline yourself and master many arts, and leave no hard problem unexamined—considering the roads open in our time, you have already come a long way!"

translations.literal: [15] Formerly you were stuck in the office of the sun.
translations.idiomatic: [15] You were long tied down in the astronomer's bureau.

translations.literal: Now again you have been restored to it.
translations.idiomatic: Now you have been sent back to that same post.

sentences.zh: [16]雖老氏曲全，進道若退，然行亦以需。

translations.literal: [16] Though the Old Master said "twisting to preserve wholeness" and "advancing in the Way seems like retreating," yet conduct also awaits its proper moment.
translations.idiomatic: [16] Laozi may counsel the crooked path that saves the whole and teach that forward motion can look like retreat, yet action still has its season.

sentences.zh: [17]必也學非所用，術有所仰，故臨川將濟，而舟鐐不存焉。

translations.literal: [17] If it must be that what you study is not what is used, and techniques have something they depend on, therefore when about to ford the river the boat and oar-locks are not there.
translations.idiomatic: [17] When what you learn is not what the world needs, and every craft waits on some outside aid, it is like standing on the bank ready to cross and finding neither boat nor oars at hand.

sentences.zh: 徒經思天衢，內昭獨智，固合理民之式也？

translations.literal: Merely to ponder in thought the heavenly thoroughfares, inwardly illumined with solitary wisdom—is that indeed the model for ordering the people?
translations.idiomatic: To brood on cosmic highways while nursing a private illumination—can that truly be the pattern for governing the people?

translations.literal: Therefore he was once slandered by mean scholars.
translations.idiomatic: Small-minded pedants have already used that against you.

sentences.zh: [18]深厲淺揭，隨時為義，曾何貪於支離，而習其孤技邪？

translations.literal: [18] Wade deep where it is deep; lift the skirts where it is shallow—adapt to the time as righteousness requires—how could one be greedy for fragmented learning and practise its solitary craft?
translations.idiomatic: [18] Wade deep where the ford is deep, hike your robe where it runs shallow, and let the hour dictate what is right—why cling to splintered doctrines and polish a single eccentric skill?

sentences.zh: [19]參[20]輪可使自轉，木雕猶能獨飛，已垂翅而還故棲，盍亦調其機而銛諸？

translations.literal: [19] Linked [20] wheels can be made to turn of themselves, and a carved wooden figure can still fly alone; you have already folded your wings and returned to your old roost—why not tune your mechanism and sharpen its edge?
translations.idiomatic: [19] Gears can be linked to spin on their own, and a wooden bird may soar without a pilot; [20] yet you fold your wings back to the same old perch—would it not be better to adjust your device and hone its blade?

translations.literal: [21] Long ago there was King Wen who sought many blessings for himself.
translations.idiomatic: [21] King Wen of old prayed only to heap blessings on himself.

translations.literal: [22] Human life rests in diligence; if you do not seek, what will you obtain?
translations.idiomatic: [22] A man's life turns on hard work; refuse to strive and you harvest nothing.

translations.literal: [23] How about humbling the body and bending the self, using fine words mutually to overcome?
translations.idiomatic: [23] Better to bow the body, humble the will, and win your point with gracious speech.

translations.literal: [24] Crying from the tall tree is then metal sound and jade vibration.
translations.idiomatic: [24] The cry from the high bough is the note of bronze struck and jade answering—true resonance.

sentences.zh: [25]用後勳，雪前吝，婞佷不柔，以意誰靳也。

translations.literal: [25] Use later merit to wipe away earlier shame; obstinate and unyielding, not pliant—with intention, who would stint?
translations.idiomatic: [25] Let fresh deeds efface old disgrace; if you stand rigid and refuse to bend, whom do you think will indulge you?

translations.literal: [26] (Commentary marker; no narrative text in the source line.)
translations.idiomatic: [26] (The line records a commentary note only.)

sentences.zh: 應之曰：是何觀同而見異也？

translations.literal: He answered, saying: What is this—viewing the same thing yet seeing it differently?
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng replied: "Why do you look at the same facts yet read them so differently?"

sentences.zh: 君子不患位之不尊，而患德之不崇；

translations.literal: The noble man does not worry that his position is not honored, but worries that his virtue is not lofty;
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman does not fret over a lack of lofty rank; he frets only when his moral stature falls short.

sentences.zh: 不恥祿之不伙，而恥智之不博。

translations.literal: he does not take shame that his salary is not large, but takes shame that his wisdom is not broad.
translations.idiomatic: He is not ashamed of a modest stipend; he is ashamed of a narrow mind.

translations.literal: [27] Therefore the arts can be studied, and conduct can be exerted by effort.
translations.idiomatic: [27] Skills can be mastered through study, and character can be forged by effort.

sentences.zh: 天爵高懸，得之在命，[28]或不速而自懷，或羨旃而不臻，[29]求之無益，故智者面而不思。

translations.literal: Heaven's nobility hangs high; obtaining it lies in fate; [28] sometimes it comes without haste yet lodges of itself, sometimes one envies the banner yet does not reach it; [29] seeking it is without benefit, therefore the wise man faces it and does not ponder.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's highest rank hangs beyond our grasp; whether we win it is fate's work. [28] Some men gain it without chasing it; others long for the pennant yet never arrive. [29] Striving buys nothing, so the wise simply meet what comes and do not brood.

sentences.zh: [30]阽身以徼幸，固貪夫之所為，未得而豫喪也。

translations.literal: [30] Risking the body to seek luck is indeed what greedy men do; before obtaining, they already lose beforehand.
translations.idiomatic: [30] To hazard life for a lucky break is the way of the grasping soul—and they often lose everything before the prize is won.

sentences.zh: [31]枉尺直尋，議者譏之，盈欲虧志，孰云非羞？

translations.literal: [31] Bending a foot to gain eight feet—discussers ridicule it; filling desire and injuring the will—who says it is not shame?
translations.idiomatic: [31] Crook an inch to win a yard, and critics will jeer; swell your appetites at the cost of your integrity, and who would call that anything but shameful?

sentences.zh: [32]於心有猜，則簋飧饌餔猶不屑餐，旌瞀以之。

translations.literal: [32] When there is suspicion in the heart, then even a basket of cooked grain and a prepared meal one still disdains to eat—such was the conduct of the starving man who refused food offered with a shout.
translations.idiomatic: [32] When misgiving clouds the mind, the kindest feast loses all savor—the lesson of the starveling who would not touch food tossed to him with a curse.

sentences.zh: [33]意之無疑，則兼金盈百而不嫌辭，孟軻以之。

translations.literal: [33] When intention is without doubt, then even a hundred pieces of doubled gold one does not disdain to decline—Meng Ke used this.
translations.idiomatic: [33] When the mind is clear, you may refuse a fortune in gold without a second thought—Mencius lived by that standard.

sentences.zh: [34]士或解裋褐而襲黼黻，或委臿築而據文軒者，度德拜爵，量績受祿也。

translations.literal: [34] Some gentlemen strip off coarse cloth and don embroidered robes; some cast aside shovel and trowel and occupy ornamented chariots—measuring virtue they receive noble rank, measuring achievement they receive salary.
translations.idiomatic: [34] Some rise from hemp jacket to brocade court dress; others lay down the builder's tools and mount lacquered carriages—rank follows proven virtue, and salary follows measured deeds.

translations.literal: [35] Contributing strength to bring about service, receiving reward must have its proper steps.
translations.idiomatic: [35] Pay out honest labor to earn your hire, and reward will climb the steps in due order.

translations.literal: [36] (Commentary marker; no narrative text in the source line.)
translations.idiomatic: [36] (The line records a commentary note only.)

sentences.zh: 渾元初基，靈軌未紀，吉凶紛錯，人用朣朦。

translations.literal: When the Primal Chaos was first founded, spiritual tracks were not yet recorded; auspicious and inauspicious were tangled in confusion, and people used them dimly.
translations.idiomatic: In the first stirrings of the cosmos no celestial pattern had yet been charted; good and ill omens mingled at random, and mankind stumbled through the dark.

translations.literal: [37] The Yellow Emperor for this was deeply grieved.
translations.idiomatic: [37] The Yellow Emperor grieved deeply over that confusion.

sentences.zh: 有風後者，是焉亮之，察三辰於上，跡禍福乎下，經緯歷數，然後天步有常，則風後之為也。

translations.literal: There was Feng Hou; he thereupon clarified it, examining the three luminaries above and tracing fortune and misfortune below, arranging the warp and weft of calendrical reckoning; thereafter the steps of heaven had constancy—then it was Feng Hou's doing.
translations.idiomatic: Then came Feng Hou, who set the pattern right: he read the three lights above, traced blessing and disaster below, and threaded the fabric of the calendar until heaven's motions ran true—that was Feng Hou's achievement.

sentences.zh: [38]當少昊清陽之末，實或亂德，人神雜擾，不可方物，重黎又相顓頊而申理之，日月即次，則重黎之為也。

translations.literal: [38] At the end of Shaohao's Qingyang reign, some there were who threw virtue into chaos; men and spirits were mixed in disorder and could not be categorized; Chong and Li again assisted Zhuanxu and straightened it; the sun and moon took their stations—then it was Chong and Li's doing.
translations.idiomatic: [38] Near the close of Shaohao's Qingyang era, some turned virtue upside down; gods and mortals mingled in chaos beyond naming. Chong and Li aided Zhuanxu to set the world straight and fixed the stations of sun and moon—that was their work.

sentences.zh: [39]人各有能，因蓺授任，鳥師別名，四叔三正，官無二業，事不並濟。

translations.literal: [39] Each man has his capacities; according to art office is assigned; bird-officers received distinct names; the four stewards and three directors—offices had no duplicate tasks, and affairs were not carried on in parallel.
translations.idiomatic: [39] Each talent fits its task, and each craft earns its appointment; bird-titled officers bore separate names, and the four guardians with the three directors kept offices from overlapping—no two bureaus did one job.

translations.literal: [40] When the day is long, then the night is short; when the sun is south, then the shadow is north.
translations.idiomatic: [40] Long daylight means a short night; when the sun stands south, shadows fall north.

translations.literal: [41] Heaven cannot even bear to combine everything—how much less can a single man encompass it.
translations.idiomatic: [41] Heaven itself cannot do every task at once—how should one mortal shoulder the whole?

sentences.zh: [42]夫玄龍，迎夏則陵雲而奮鱗，樂時也；

translations.literal: [42] As for the dark dragon, welcoming summer then mounts the clouds and flails its scales—it is rejoicing in the season;
translations.idiomatic: [42] Consider the black dragon: in summer it climbs the mist and shakes out its scales, delighting in the season;

sentences.zh: 涉冬則淈泥而潛蟠，避害也。

translations.literal: crossing winter then it wallows in mud and coils hidden—it is avoiding harm.
translations.idiomatic: in winter it burrows into the mire and coils deep below, fleeing harm.

sentences.zh: [43]公旦道行，故制典禮以尹天下，懼教誨之不從，有人［之］不理。

translations.literal: [43] The Duke of Zhou's way was practiced, therefore he instituted canonical rites to govern the realm, fearing that instruction might not be obeyed and that there would be people not put in order.
translations.idiomatic: [43] When the Duke of Zhou's teaching prevailed, he framed the ritual code to rule the world, dreading lest the people ignore his lessons or live beyond the reach of government.

sentences.zh: [44]仲尼不遇，故論六經以俟來辟，[45]恥一物之不知，有事之無范。

translations.literal: [44] Zhongni did not meet his time, therefore he discussed the Six Classics to await later enlightened rulers, [45] taking shame that one thing was unknown, that there were affairs without models.
translations.idiomatic: [44] Confucius, denied a king's ear, set the Six Classics in order for rulers yet to come, [45] ashamed to leave any topic unexplained or any duty without a pattern.

translations.literal: What they examined was not uniform; how could they be made one?
translations.idiomatic: Their inquiries took different paths—how could one law force them into a single mold?

translations.literal: [46] (Commentary marker; no narrative text in the source line.)
translations.idiomatic: [46] (The line records a commentary note only.)

sentences.zh: 夫戰國交爭，戎車競驅，君若綴旒，人無所麗。

translations.literal: In the Warring States they crossed swords in rivalry; war chariots raced in pursuit; the lord was like a tassel on a banner, and the people had nothing to cling to.
translations.idiomatic: When the warring states clashed, chariots thundered after one another and kings dangled like ornaments on a staff, while the people had no one to hold to.

sentences.zh: [47]燭武縣縋而秦伯退師，[48]魯連繫箭而聊城□柝。

translations.literal: [47] Zhu Zhiwu was let down by rope and the Earl of Qin withdrew his army; [48] Lu Lian tied on an arrow and at Liaocheng the watchman's clapper ceased.
translations.idiomatic: [47] Zhu Zhiwu was lowered on a rope and the Qin army turned back; [48] Lu Lian's letter, shot over the wall on an arrow, stilled the drum at Liaocheng.

sentences.zh: [49]從往則合，橫來則離，安危無常，要在說夫。

translations.literal: [49] If one went with alliance they united; if transverse forces came they separated; safety and danger had no constancy—the crux lay in persuading the men.
translations.idiomatic: [49] Join the vertical league and states cohered; yield to the horizontal pact and they split apart—fortune and ruin shifted overnight, and everything turned on whose tongue won the day.

translations.literal: [50] All took obtaining men as excellence and losing gentlemen as fault.
translations.idiomatic: [50] Every court knew that winning the right men spelled success and losing them spelled blame.

translations.literal: [51] Therefore Fan Kuai tore aside the curtain and entered to see Gaozu;
translations.idiomatic: [51] Fan Kuai swept the tent aside and forced his way in to warn Gaozu;

translations.literal: [52] Gaozu squatted on the wash-basin dais in answering Master Li.
translations.idiomatic: [52] Liu Bang sat sprawled on his foot-washing stool while he answered Li Yiji—rough manners that won the realm.

translations.literal: [53] At this juncture it was like the soft-shell turtle crying and something answering.
translations.idiomatic: [53] In such moments one bold voice could rouse the whole camp, as a turtle's cry draws its answering chorus.

sentences.zh: [54]故能同心戮力，勤恤人隱，[55]奄受區夏，遂定帝位，皆謀臣之由也。

translations.literal: [54] Therefore they were able with one heart to exhaust their strength, diligently caring for the people's hardships, [55] suddenly receiving the central realm and thereupon fixing the imperial position—all from the planning ministers.
translations.idiomatic: [54] That is how they could unite in one purpose, spare no effort for the people's woes, [55] seize the heartland of the realm, and set the imperial throne—all through the counsel of wise ministers.

sentences.zh: 故一介之策，各有攸建，子長諜之，爛然有第。

translations.literal: Therefore each single-straw plan had its proper establishment; Zichang spied them out, and they were brilliantly ordered.
translations.idiomatic: Each modest stratagem had its appointed place, and Sima Qian mapped them until the whole tapestry shone in order.

sentences.zh: [56]夫女魃北而應龍翔，洪鼎聲而軍容息；

translations.literal: [56] When the drought demon fled north, the responding dragon soared; when the great cauldron sounded, military display rested;
translations.idiomatic: [56] When the drought goddess fled north, the rain dragon rose; when the sacred cauldron tolled, the armies stood down;

sentences.zh: [57]溽暑至而鶉火棲，寒冰冱而黿鼉蟄。

translations.literal: [57] when sweltering summer arrives, the Quail Fire lodges; when ice freezes hard, the soft-shell turtles and alligators coil.
translations.idiomatic: [57] sultry heat drives the Quail Fire constellation to its rest, and bitter cold sends turtles and alligators into hibernation.

sentences.zh: [58]今也，皇澤宣洽，海外混同，萬方億丑，並質共劑，若修成之不暇，尚何功之可立！

translations.literal: [58] Now, imperial grace spreads and harmonizes; beyond the seas there is unification; the myriad regions and hundred million kinds all offer substance and share the compound; if completing cultivation is not yet finished, how can any merit yet be established!
translations.idiomatic: [58] Today the emperor's kindness floods every corner, the outer seas share one rule, and countless lands and peoples blend their talents in common service—when even self-cultivation is barely finished, what room is left for boasting of deeds?

translations.literal: [59] Establishing affairs has three parts; speech is the lowest row;
translations.idiomatic: [59] Great undertakings rest on three things, and fine words rank last among them;

sentences.zh: 下列且不可庶矣，奚冀其二哉！

translations.literal: the lowest row is not yet to be hoped for—how can one hope for its second!
translations.idiomatic: if the lowest rung is still beyond reach, how dare we dream of the higher ones?

translations.literal: [60] (Commentary marker; no narrative text in the source line.)
translations.idiomatic: [60] (The line records a commentary note only.)

sentences.zh: 於茲搢紳如雲，儒士成林，及津者風攄，失塗者幽僻，遭遇難要，趨偶為幸。

translations.literal: At this time girdle-and-pleat officials were like clouds, Confucian scholars formed a forest; those who reached the ford were like wind scattering, those who missed the path were secluded in darkness; encountering fortune was hard to require; rushing by chance was fortune.
translations.idiomatic: In such an age officials swarmed like clouds and scholars stood thick as a forest: those who caught the current shot forward like windblown spray, while those who missed the channel moldered in shadow—luck was a rare appointment, and most who rose did so by sheer accident.

sentences.zh: 世易俗異，事埶舛殊，不能通其變，而一度以揆之，[61]斯契船而求□，守株而伺兔也。

translations.literal: The age changes and customs differ; affairs and circumstances are contrary and different; if one cannot comprehend their changes, yet with one measure gauges them, [61] this is like notching the boat to seek a sword, or guarding a stump waiting for a rabbit.
translations.idiomatic: Times shift and manners alter; if you refuse to change with them yet measure every case with one old yardstick, [61] you are only marking the boat to find a lost sword or watching a tree stump for a stray hare.

sentences.zh: [62]冒愧逞願，必無仁以繼之，有道者所不履也。

translations.literal: [62] Braving shame to indulge one's wishes must lack humaneness to follow it; men of the Way do not tread such a path.
translations.idiomatic: [62] To swallow shame for the sake of appetite is to abandon humaneness altogether—the man of principle will not walk that road.

sentences.zh: 越王句踐事此，故厥緒不永。

translations.literal: The Yue king Goujian acted on this; therefore his line was not long-lasting.
translations.idiomatic: King Goujian of Yue took that path, and his dynasty's fortune soon ran out.

sentences.zh: [63]快捷方式邪至，我不忍以投步；

translations.literal: [63] Crooked shortcuts and evil arrivals—I cannot bear to set foot on them with hurrying steps;
translations.idiomatic: [63] I cannot bring myself to hurry along crooked byways to wrongful gain;

sentences.zh: 干進苟容，我不忍以歙肩。

translations.literal: pushing for advancement and shamelessly accommodating—I cannot bear to shrug my shoulders for them.
translations.idiomatic: nor will I shrug and wriggle forward by flattering those in power.

sentences.zh: [64]雖有犀舟勁鐐，猶人涉卬否，有須者也。

translations.literal: [64] Although there are rhinoceros-hide boats and strong oar-locks, still it is like "others ford it, I alone do not"—there is something to wait for.
translations.idiomatic: [64] Even with the stoutest hull and oars, I would rather stand on the bank like the man in the ode who says, "They cross, but not I"—some things are worth waiting for."

sentences.zh: [65]姑亦奉順敦篤，守以忠信，得之不休，不獲不吝。

translations.literal: [65] For the nonce I also respectfully follow earnest sincerity, guard with loyalty and trust, not rest if I obtain, not be stingy if I do not obtain.
translations.idiomatic: [65] For now I mean to keep to modest earnestness, hold fast to loyalty and good faith, rejoice without excess when I succeed, and bear failure without bitterness.

sentences.zh: [66]不見是而不惛，居下位而不憂，允上德之常服焉。

translations.literal: [66] Not seeing approval yet not being dim, occupying a low position yet not worrying—this is truly the constant garment of highest virtue.
translations.idiomatic: [66] To lack recognition yet stay clear-minded, to hold a low post yet stay untroubled—there is the everyday robe of the highest virtue.

sentences.zh: [67]方將師天老而友地典，與之乎高睨而大談，孔甲且不足慕，焉稱殷彭及周聃！

translations.literal: [67] I am about to take as teacher Tian Lao and as friend Di Dian, and with them look down from on high and discourse greatly—Kong Jia is not yet worth admiring, how mention Yin Peng and Zhou Dan!
translations.idiomatic: [67] I would sooner make Tian Lao my master and Di Dian my companions, then lift my gaze and speak freely among them—Kong Jia is hardly worth envying, let alone the likes of Pengzu or Dan, prince of Zhou!

translations.literal: [68] Having skills different from the age, therefore solitude is what is sought.
translations.idiomatic: [68] When your craft does not match the world's fashion, you must seek your path alone.

sentences.zh: [69]子憂朱泙曼之無所用，吾恨輪扁之無所教也。

translations.literal: [69] You worry that Zhu Pingman has no use for his skill; I regret that Wheelwright Bian has nothing to teach.
translations.idiomatic: [69] You fret that Zhu Pingman's art finds no outlet; I mourn that Wheelwright Bian had no pupil worthy of his wheel's secret.

sentences.zh: [70]子鶯木雕獨飛，□我垂翅故棲，吾感去□附鴟，悲爾先笑而後號也。

translations.literal: [70] You praise the carved wooden bird that flies alone; I fold my wings to my old roost; I pity how you leave the proper perch to cling to owls—I grieve that you first laugh and afterward will wail.
translations.idiomatic: [70] You exalt a wooden automaton that flutters by itself, while I tuck my wings back to the nest I know; I pity anyone who forsakes the true roost to ride with owls—how sad that you laugh now yet will soon be wailing.

translations.literal: [71] (Commentary marker; no narrative text in the source line.)
translations.idiomatic: [71] (The line records a commentary note only.)

sentences.zh: 斐豹以斃督燔書，禮至以掖國作銘；

translations.literal: Fei Bao by killing the supervisor burned the document; Li Zhi by supporting the state made an inscription;
translations.idiomatic: Fei Bao slew his overseer and burned the pledge; Li Zhi steadied the state and cast a commemorative inscription;

sentences.zh: [72]弦高以牛餼退敵，墨翟以縈帶全城；

translations.literal: [72] Xian Gao with oxen as rations made the enemy withdraw; Mozi with encircling belts saved a whole city;
translations.idiomatic: [72] Xian Gao drove cattle as gifts and turned the army back; Mozi looped girdle-cords and saved a besieged town;

sentences.zh: [73]貫高以端辭顯義，蘇武以禿節效貞；

translations.literal: [73] Guan Gao by upright words displayed righteousness; Su Wu by his hairless staff displayed fidelity;
translations.idiomatic: [73] Guan Gao spoke straight and made justice visible; Su Wu clutched his bare staff and proved his loyalty;

sentences.zh: [74]蒱且以飛矰逞巧，詹何以沉鉤致精；

translations.literal: [74] Pu Ju by flying string-bolts displayed skill; Zhan He by sinking hooks attained refinement;
translations.idiomatic: [74] Pu Ju showed his art with a whistling crossbow bolt; Zhan He fished the depths with a submerged hook;

sentences.zh: [75]弈秋以潟局取譽，王豹以清謳流聲。

translations.literal: [75] Yi Qiu by a leaking board gained fame; Wang Bao by clear songs let sound flow.
translations.idiomatic: [75] Yi Qiu won renown at the dripping chessboard; Wang Bao made his name with pure, ringing song.

sentences.zh: [76]僕進不能參名於二立，退又不能腢彼數子。

translations.literal: [76] I, in advancing, cannot join my name among the two establishments; in retreating, I cannot rank with those several men.
translations.idiomatic: [76] Advancing, I cannot stand beside the twin pillars of merit; falling back, I cannot match those paragons either.

sentences.zh: [77]愍三墳之既頹，惜八索之不理。

translations.literal: [77] I grieve that the Three Mounds have already collapsed; I regret that the Eight Searches are not put in order.
translations.idiomatic: [77] I mourn how the Three Mounds lie in ruin and lament that the Eight Searches lie uncatalogued.

sentences.zh: [78]庶前訓之可鑽，聊朝隱乎柱史。

translations.literal: [78] I hope the former instructions can be drilled into; for the nonce I morning-hide as pillar-scribe.
translations.idiomatic: [78] I would still study the teachings of the ancients and, for now, play the hermit clerk by the court pillar.

sentences.zh: [79]且韞櫝以待價，踵顏氏以行止。

translations.literal: [79] Moreover I wrap the gem in its case waiting for a price; I follow the Yan clan in walking and stopping.
translations.idiomatic: [79] Like a jewel left in its casket until a buyer appears, I take my cue from the Yan family in when to go and when to stay.

sentences.zh: [80]曾不慊夫晉、楚，敢告誠於知己。

translations.literal: [80] I have never been discontented with Jin and Chu; I dare announce sincerity to one who knows me.
translations.idiomatic: [80] I do not resent the great states of Jin and Chu; I speak my heart plainly to a friend who understands.

translations.literal: [81] (Commentary marker; no narrative text in the source line.)
translations.idiomatic: [81] (The line records a commentary note only.)

sentences.zh: 陽嘉元年，復造候風地動儀。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yangjia, he again made a wind-indicating seismoscope.
translations.idiomatic: In Yangjia 1 (132 CE) he built a new instrument to sense the wind and detect earthquakes.

sentences.zh: 以精銅鑄成，員徑八尺，合蓋隆起，形似酒尊，飾以篆文山龜鳥獸之形。

translations.literal: It was cast from fine bronze; its circular diameter was eight chi; the joined cover rose in a dome; its shape resembled a wine jar; it was ornamented with seal-script mountains, turtles, birds, and beasts.
translations.idiomatic: He cast it from refined bronze eight chi across, with a domed lid like a ritual wine-jar incised with seal-script hills, tortoises, birds, and beasts.

sentences.zh: 中有都柱，傍行八道，施關發機。

translations.literal: Inside there was a central post; eight channels ran along the sides; triggers and releases were installed.
translations.idiomatic: A thick central column rose inside, with eight channels round it fitted with catches and release levers.

sentences.zh: 外有八龍，首銜銅丸，下有蟾蜍，張口承之。

translations.literal: Outside there were eight dragons; their mouths held bronze balls; below were toads with mouths open to receive them.
translations.idiomatic: Eight bronze dragons ringed the vessel, each gripping a ball in its jaws, while below them open-mouthed toads waited to catch the drops.

sentences.zh: [82]其牙機巧制，皆隱在尊中，覆蓋周密無際。

translations.literal: [82] Their gear-teeth and ingenious mechanism were all hidden inside the jar; the cover fitted so tightly there was no gap.
translations.idiomatic: [82] The intricate trigger-work lay hidden within the jar, and the lid sealed the whole so tightly that not a crack showed.

sentences.zh: 如有地動，尊則振龍機發吐丸，而蟾蜍銜之。

translations.literal: If there were an earthquake, the jar then shook, the dragon mechanism released and spat out the ball, and the toad caught it in its mouth.
translations.idiomatic: When a tremor passed through the earth, the vessel shuddered, the dragon jaws sprang open and spat their balls, and the toads caught them.

sentences.zh: 振聲激揚，伺者因此覺知。

translations.literal: The vibrating sound was sharp and loud; observers thereby became aware.
translations.idiomatic: The clang rang sharp and clear, so attendants knew at once that the device had fired.

sentences.zh: 雖一龍發機，而七首不動，尋其方面，乃知震之所在。

translations.literal: Although only one dragon released its mechanism, the seven heads did not move; seeking its direction, one then knew the place where the shock occurred.
translations.idiomatic: Only the dragon facing the quake moved; the other seven stayed still, so from the active dragon alone one could read the bearing of the epicenter.

translations.literal: When verified by events, it matched like a spirit.
translations.idiomatic: Time after time, real earthquakes proved the reading uncannily exact.

translations.literal: From what is recorded in documents, there has never been such a thing.
translations.idiomatic: Nothing like it appears in any earlier record.

sentences.zh: 嘗一龍機發而地不覺動，京師學者咸怪其無征，後數日驛至，果地震隴西，於是皆服其妙。

translations.literal: Once one dragon mechanism released but the earth was not felt to move; scholars in the capital all wondered that there was no verification; after several days a post-rider arrived, and indeed there had been an earthquake in Longxi; thereupon all submitted to its subtlety.
translations.idiomatic: Once a dragon fired though no one in Luoyang had felt a tremor, and the capital savants scoffed at a false alarm—until couriers brought news days later of a quake in Longxi; then they conceded its uncanny precision.

sentences.zh: 自此以後，乃令史官記地動所從方起。

translations.literal: From this time onward, he then ordered the historian officials to record from which direction earthquakes began.
translations.idiomatic: After that the court directed the historiographers to log the compass direction from which each shock originated.

sentences.zh: 時政事漸損，權移於下，衡因上疏陳事曰：「伏惟陛下宣哲克明，繼體承天，中遭傾覆，龍德泥蟠。

translations.literal: At that time government affairs gradually declined, power shifted downward, and Heng therefore submitted a memorial stating affairs, saying: "I bow my head and consider that Your Majesty is enlightened and wise, inheriting the bodily virtue and receiving Heaven; in the midst you encountered overturn; the dragon virtue lay coiled in the mud.
translations.idiomatic: As administration slid into decay and real authority slipped to subordinates, Zhang Heng laid before the throne a memorial that began: "Your Majesty combines sagely insight with Heaven's mandate, yet the realm once knew upheaval and Your dragon virtue was forced to lie coiled in the mire."

sentences.zh: [83]今乘雲高躋，盤桓天位，誠所謂將隆大位，必先倥□之也。

translations.literal: [83] Now mounting the clouds and ascending on high, you turn about in the celestial seat; it is truly what is called: when about to elevate to a great position, one must first suffer distress.
translations.idiomatic: [83] Now You ride the clouds to the height of the throne; the proverb is true—the way to a great station always passes through hardship first.

sentences.zh: [84]親履艱難者知下情，備經險易者達物偽。

translations.literal: [84] Those who personally tread hardship know conditions below; those fully experienced in danger and ease comprehend the falseness of things.
translations.idiomatic: [84] He who has walked through hardship knows the mood of the people; he who has tasted peril and ease sees through worldly sham.

sentences.zh: [85]故能一貫萬機，靡所疑惑，百揆允當，庶績咸熙。

translations.literal: [85] Therefore he can thread through the myriad threads of government without doubt; the hundred duties are fittingly settled; all tasks flourish.
translations.idiomatic: [85] That is why You can thread every strand of rule without confusion, set every office aright, and bring every task to flourishing order.

sentences.zh: 宜獲福祉神祇，受譽黎庶。

translations.literal: It is fitting to obtain blessing from the spirits, and receive praise from the black-haired people.
translations.idiomatic: You should win the blessing of the gods and the praise of the common folk.

sentences.zh: 而陰陽未和，□眚屢見，神明幽遠，冥鑒在茲。

translations.literal: Yet yin and yang are not harmonized; omens appear repeatedly; the spirits are remote and dark; their silent mirror is here.
translations.idiomatic: Yet yin and yang stay out of tune, ill omens keep appearing, and though the spirits seem distant, their silent judgment falls here and now.

sentences.zh: 福仁禍淫，景響而應，因德降休，乘失致咎，天道雖遠，吉凶可見，近世鄭、蔡、江、樊、周廣、王聖，皆為效矣。

translations.literal: Fortune follows humaneness and calamity follows licentiousness; shadow and echo respond; according to virtue blessings descend, riding on loss fault arrives; though the Way of Heaven is distant, auspiciousness and inauspiciousness can be seen; in recent generations Zheng, Cai, Jiang, Fan, Zhou Guang, and Wang Sheng all served as proofs.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven rewards humaneness and punishes excess; cause and effect answer like shadow and echo. Good conduct draws grace, misrule summons disaster. The Way of Heaven is high, yet its signs are plain: look at Zheng, Cai, Jiang, Fan, Zhou Guang, and Wang Sheng in our own day.

sentences.zh: [86]故恭儉畏忌，必蒙祉祚，奢淫諂慢，鮮不夷戮，前事不忘，後事之師也。

translations.literal: [86] Therefore reverent frugality and cautious awe surely receive blessing; extravagance, licentiousness, flattery, and arrogance rarely escape slaughter; not forgetting former affairs is the teacher of later affairs.
translations.idiomatic: [86] Reverent thrift and sober awe win Heaven's favor, while luxury, lust, flattery, and swagger invite the axe. The past, if not forgotten, becomes the mirror of the future.

sentences.zh: 夫情勝其性，流遯忘反，[87]豈唯不肖，中才皆然。

translations.literal: As for emotion overcoming nature, flowing away and forgetting to return, [87] it is not only the unworthy; men of middling talent are all thus.
translations.idiomatic: When passion masters one's true nature and drifts on without return, [87] it is not only the worthless who fall—most men of middling gifts do the same.

sentences.zh: 苟非大賢，不能見得思義，故積惡成釁，罪不可解也。

translations.literal: If one is not a great worthy, one cannot on seeing gain think of righteousness; therefore accumulated evil becomes disaster, and guilt cannot be dissolved.
translations.idiomatic: Without the virtue of a sage, few can see profit and still recall duty; small sins pile into a charge that no plea can wipe away.

sentences.zh: 向使能瞻前顧後，援鏡自戒，則何陷於凶患乎！

translations.literal: If only one could look before and behind, take the mirror and warn oneself—then how would one fall into calamity!
translations.idiomatic: Had they only looked ahead and behind and taken the mirror of history as warning, they would never have tumbled into ruin.

sentences.zh: [88]貴寵之臣，觿所屬仰，其有愆尤，上下知之。

translations.literal: [88] As for favored ministers, on them all eyes are fixed; if they have faults, above and below know them.
translations.idiomatic: [88] Favored courtiers live under every eye; their slightest slip is known above and below.

sentences.zh: □美譏惡，有心皆同，故怨讟溢乎四海，神明降其禍辟也。

translations.literal: Praising the good and blaming the evil—all hearts are the same; therefore resentment overflows within the four seas, and the spirits send down their punishments.
translations.idiomatic: All men join in praising good and condemning evil, so when rage fills the realm the spirits answer with strokes of fate.

sentences.zh: [89]頃年雨常不足，思求所失，則《洪範》所謂『僭恆陽若』者也。

translations.literal: [89] In recent years rain has often been insufficient; seeking what was lost, then it is what the Hong fan calls "presumption brings constant yang like this."
translations.idiomatic: [89] Rain has failed too often of late. Searching for the fault, we meet the Hong fan warning that arrogance brings relentless drought.

sentences.zh: [90]懼腢臣奢侈，昏踰典式，自下逼上，用速咎徵。

translations.literal: [90] I fear that ministers are extravagant, blindly transgressing models and patterns, pressing upward from below, thereby hastening signs of blame.
translations.idiomatic: [90] I fear ministers grow lavish, trample the statutes, and push upward from below, hastening Heaven's rebukes.

sentences.zh: 又前年京師地震土裂，[91]裂者威分，震者人擾也。

translations.literal: Moreover in the preceding year in the capital there was an earthquake and the earth split; [91] splitting means power divided; earthquake means the people disturbed.
translations.idiomatic: Last year the capital shook and the ground gaped: [91] a rift means authority split; a tremor means the people are unsettled.

sentences.zh: 君以靜唱，臣以動和，威自上出，不趣於下，禮之政也。

translations.literal: The ruler by stillness leads the call; ministers by movement harmonize; awe issues from above and is not hastened from below—this is the government of ritual.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign sets the tone in stillness; ministers answer in motion; majesty must flow from the throne, not be tugged up from below—that is the way of ritual government.

sentences.zh: 竊懼聖思厭倦，制不專己，恩不忍割，與觿共威。

translations.literal: I privately fear that sagely thoughts may weary, that regulation may not be kept in Your sole hands, that kindness may be unwilling to cut off, and that You share awe with the masses.
translations.idiomatic: I fear Your mind may tire of detail, that edicts may slip from Your sole grip, that mercy may shrink from cutting ties, and that You let ministers share Your awe.

translations.literal: Awe cannot be divided; virtue cannot be shared.
translations.idiomatic: Majesty admits no partner, and moral authority cannot be parceled out.

sentences.zh: 《洪範》曰：『臣有作威作福玉食，害於而家，凶於而國。

translations.literal: The Hong fan says: "If ministers make awe, make blessing, and jade food, it harms your house and is inauspicious for your state."
translations.idiomatic: The Hong fan warns: when ministers wield terror and favor and feast like kings, the harm begins in the royal house and ends in the ruin of the state.

sentences.zh: 』天鑒孔明，雖□不失，□異示人，前後數矣，而未見所革，以復往悔。

translations.literal: Heaven's mirror is supremely bright; though there is loss it does not miss; showing strange signs to men, front and back several times already, yet one has not seen what is reformed, thereby repeating past regrets.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's mirror misses nothing; time after time it has shown prodigies to the world, yet nothing changes and old regrets repeat themselves.

translations.literal: [92] Unless one is a sage, one cannot be without fault.
translations.idiomatic: [92] Only a sage is free of fault.

sentences.zh: 願陛下思惟所以稽古率舊，勿令刑德八柄，不由天子。

translations.literal: I hope Your Majesty will ponder how to examine antiquity and follow the old, and not let the eight handles of punishments and virtues fail to proceed from the Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: I beg You to study antiquity, hold fast to ancient precedent, and never let the eight handles of reward and punishment slip from the Son of Heaven's hand.

sentences.zh: [93]若恩從上下，事依禮制，禮制修則奢僭息，事合宜則無凶咎。

translations.literal: [93] If favor proceeds from high to low and affairs accord with ritual pattern, when ritual pattern is cultivated then extravagance and presumption cease; when affairs fit what is right then there is no inauspicious blame.
translations.idiomatic: [93] When grace flows from above and every act fits the rites, excess ends and ill omens fade.

sentences.zh: 然後神望允塞，□消不至矣。」

translations.literal: Then the spirits' expectations will be filled; omens will dissolve and not arrive."
translations.idiomatic: Then the spirits will be satisfied and calamitous signs will trouble the court no more."

sentences.zh: 初，光武善讖，及顯宗、肅宗因祖述焉。

translations.literal: At first Guangwu favored prognostication texts, and Emperor Ming and Emperor Zhang therefore transmitted and continued them.
translations.idiomatic: From the start Emperor Guangwu trusted apocryphal prophecy, and Emperors Ming and Zhang carried on the tradition.

sentences.zh: 自中興之後，儒者爭學圖緯，兼復附以訞言。

translations.literal: From the restoration onward, Confucian scholars vied to study charts and weft-texts, again attaching deceptive words.
translations.idiomatic: After the restoration, scholars rushed to master the charts and weft-books, piling fraudulent glosses upon them.

sentences.zh: 衡以圖緯虛妄，非聖人之法，乃上疏曰：

translations.literal: Heng considered charts and weft-texts empty and false, not the law of the sages, and therefore submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng judged those writings hollow forgeries, unworthy of the sages, and laid before the throne a memorial that read:

sentences.zh: 臣聞聖人明審律歷以定吉凶，重之以卜筮，雜之以九宮，[94]經天驗道，本盡於此。

translations.literal: I have heard that sages clearly examine statutes and calendars to fix auspiciousness and inauspiciousness, emphasize them with tortoise and milfoil divination, mix them with the nine palaces, [94] thread heaven and verify the Way—the root is exhausted in this.
translations.idiomatic: The sages fixed good and ill omen through statutes and calendars, checked them with tortoise and milfoil, and crossed them with the nine-palace scheme; [94] that is how they read Heaven—there the method ends.

sentences.zh: 或觀星辰逆順，寒燠所由，或察龜策之占，巫覡之言，[95]其所因者，非一術也。

translations.literal: Some observe the stars' contrary or compliant motion and cold and heat's causes; some inspect tortoise and milfoil divinations and shamans' words; [95] what they rely on is not one technique.
translations.idiomatic: Some watch the courses of the stars and the seasons of cold and heat; others study shell and stalk divinations or the mutterings of mediums; [95] their tools are many, not one.

sentences.zh: 立言於前，有征於後，故智者貴焉，謂之讖書。

translations.literal: They set forth words beforehand, and afterward there was verification; therefore the wise prized them and called them prognostication books.
translations.idiomatic: Their words were uttered first and verified later, so the wise honored them as prophecy.

translations.literal: When prognostication books first appeared, those who knew them were few.
translations.idiomatic: When those books first appeared, almost no one had seen them.

sentences.zh: 自漢取秦，用兵力戰，功成業遂，可謂大事，當此之時，莫或稱讖。

translations.literal: From the time Han took Qin by military force in battle, merit was completed and enterprise achieved—it can be called a great affair; at that time none spoke of prognostication.
translations.idiomatic: When Han conquered Qin by arms and the great work was done, no one cited prophecy to explain it.

sentences.zh: 若夏侯勝、眭孟之徒，以道術立名，其所述著，無讖一言。

translations.literal: Men such as Xiahou Sheng and Sui Meng established their names by the Way and techniques; what they wrote contained not one word of prognostication.
translations.idiomatic: Thinkers like Xiahou Sheng and Sui Meng built reputations on true learning, and their writings never mention weft-text prophecy.

sentences.zh: 劉向父子領校秘書，閱定九流，亦無讖錄。

translations.literal: Liu Xiang and his son supervised collation of the secretariat books, reviewed and fixed the nine currents, and likewise had no prognostication records.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiang and Liu Xin ran the imperial library, sorted the nine schools, and found no genuine prophetic canon there either.

translations.literal: After Cheng and Ai, only then did one begin to hear of them.
translations.idiomatic: Not until the reigns of Cheng and Ai did such texts begin to circulate.

sentences.zh: [96]《尚書》堯使迺理洪水，九載績用不成，迺則殛死，禹乃嗣興。

translations.literal: [96] The Documents says Yao ordered Gun to regulate the flood; after nine years the achievement was not completed, then he executed Gun, and Yu then rose in succession.
translations.idiomatic: [96] The Documents record how Yao charged Gun with the flood, executed him after nine fruitless years, and raised Yu in his place.

translations.literal: [97] Yet the Spring and Autumn prognostication says 'Gong Gong regulated the water.'
translations.idiomatic: [97] A Spring and Autumn weft-text instead claims Gong Gong managed the waters—a flat contradiction.

sentences.zh: 凡讖皆云黃帝伐蚩尤，而詩讖獨以為『蚩尤敗，然後堯受命』。

translations.literal: All prognostications say the Yellow Emperor attacked Chiyou, but the Poetry prognostication alone says "Chiyou was defeated, and then Yao received the mandate."
translations.idiomatic: Every apocryphon names the Yellow Emperor smiting Chiyou, yet the Poetry weft alone says Chiyou fell first and only then did Yao receive Heaven's charge.

sentences.zh: 春秋元命包中有公輸班與墨翟，事見戰國，非春秋時也。

translations.literal: In the Spring and Autumn Yuanming bao there are Gongshu Ban and Mozi; the events appear in the Warring States period, not the Spring and Autumn period.
translations.idiomatic: The Yuanming bao lists Gongshu Ban and Mozi—figures of the Warring States, not the Spring and Autumn era.

translations.literal: [98] It also says 'there is separately a Yizhou.'
translations.idiomatic: [98] Another line speaks of a separate Yizhou commandery.

translations.literal: The establishment of Yizhou lay in Han times.
translations.idiomatic: Yizhou was not organized as a commandery until the Han dynasty.

translations.literal: [99] Its naming of the three assistants' various tombs—the generations can be known.
translations.idiomatic: [99] When it names the tombs of the metropolitan region, the generations it names are matters we can check.

translations.literal: As for the charts, they end at Emperor Cheng.
translations.idiomatic: The charts themselves stop at Emperor Cheng's reign.

sentences.zh: 一卷之書，互異數事，聖人之言，埶無若是，殆必虛偽之徒，以要世取資。

translations.literal: In one scroll's book, several matters contradict one another; the words of the sages have no such tendency—surely these are false men who seek to gain resources from the age.
translations.idiomatic: One slim scroll contradicts itself in a dozen places; no sage ever wrote like that—these are forgers fishing for patronage.

sentences.zh: 往者侍中賈逵摘讖互異三十餘事，諸言讖者皆不能說。

translations.literal: Formerly Palace Attendant Jia Kui plucked out more than thirty mutually contradictory items from the prognostications, and all who spoke for prophecy could not explain them.
translations.idiomatic: Palace Attendant Jia Kui once listed over thirty flat contradictions in the weft-books, and no defender of prophecy could answer him.

sentences.zh: 至於王莽篡位，漢世大禍，八十篇何為不戒？

translations.literal: As for Wang Mang's usurpation of the throne, the great calamity of the Han house—why did the eighty chapters not warn?
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Mang seized the throne and brought catastrophe on Han, why did those eighty chapters of prophecy utter no warning?

sentences.zh: 則知圖讖成於哀平之際也。

translations.literal: Then one knows that charts and prognostications were completed at the border of Ai and Ping.
translations.idiomatic: That shows the charts and wefts were forged around the Ai and Ping reigns.

sentences.zh: 且河洛、六蓺，篇錄已定，後人皮傅，無所容篡。

translations.literal: Moreover the River Charts, Luo Writings, and six arts—their chapter lists are already fixed; later men superficially attach and have no room to interpolate.
translations.idiomatic: The River Chart, Luo Writing, and the six classical arts already have fixed tables of contents; later hacks cannot slip new lines into them.

sentences.zh: [100]永元中，清河宋景遂以歷紀推言水□，而偽稱洞視玉版。

translations.literal: [100] In the Yongyuan era, Song Jing of Qinghe used calendrical reckoning to push and speak of water omens, and falsely claimed clairvoyant jade tablets.
translations.idiomatic: [100] During Yongyuan, Song Jing of Qinghe spun calendar lore into predictions of flood omens and forged tales of spirit-revealed jade slips.

translations.literal: [101] Some even to the point of ruining family property entered mountain forests.
translations.idiomatic: [101] Some ruined their estates and fled to the hills chasing such visions.

sentences.zh: 後皆無效，而復采前世成事，以為證驗。

translations.literal: Afterward all were without effect, yet again they gathered former-age completed events to serve as verification.
translations.idiomatic: When every omen failed, they ransacked old annals and pretended the texts had predicted them.

sentences.zh: 至於永建復統，則不能知。

translations.literal: As for the Yongjian restoration of the imperial line, then they could not know it.
translations.idiomatic: The Yongjian restoration of Han caught them completely by surprise.

sentences.zh: [102]此皆欺世罔俗，以昧埶位，情偽較然，莫之糾禁。

translations.literal: [102] All these deceive the world and delude custom in order to darken their way to office; the truth of the fraud is clear, yet none corrects or forbids them.
translations.idiomatic: [102] Such men cheat the world to win posts, and though the fraud is obvious no one restrains them.

sentences.zh: 且律歷、卦候、九宮、風角，數有征效，世莫肯學，而競稱不佔之書。

translations.literal: Moreover statutes and calendars, hexagram oracles, nine palaces, and wind corners have numbers with verifiable effects; the age is unwilling to study them, yet competes to praise books that need no divination.
translations.idiomatic: Statutes, calendars, hexagram divination, nine palaces, and wind-corner astrology all yield testable results, yet scholars shun them to chase books that never need to be proven.

sentences.zh: [103]譬猶畫工，惡圖犬馬而好作鬼魅，誠以實事難形，而虛偽不窮也。

translations.literal: [103] It is like painters who hate to depict dogs and horses but love to paint ghosts and demons—truly because real things are hard to shape, while falsehood knows no end.
translations.idiomatic: [103] It is like painters who shun dogs and horses yet lavish ink on ghosts—truth is hard to draw, lies are endless.

sentences.zh: [104]宜收藏圖讖，一禁絕之，則朱紫無所眩，典籍無瑕玷矣。

translations.literal: [104] It is fitting to collect and store charts and prognostications and uniformly forbid them; then red and purple will have nothing to dazzle, and the classics will be without blemish.
translations.idiomatic: [104] Seize the apocrypha, forbid them outright, and the false will no longer stain the true classics.

sentences.zh: 後遷侍中，帝引在帷幄，諷議左右。

translations.literal: Later he was promoted to Palace Attendant; the emperor drew him into the curtain and had him offer indirect remonstrance at his side.
translations.idiomatic: Promoted to Palace Attendant, he was summoned into the imperial curtains to speak frankly at the emperor's ear.

translations.literal: He once asked Heng what the realm most hated.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor once asked Zhang Heng what evils most plagued the land.

sentences.zh: 宦官懼其毀己，皆共目之，衡乃詭對而出。

translations.literal: The eunuchs feared he would slander them; they all stared at him together; Heng then answered deceitfully and departed.
translations.idiomatic: The eunuchs feared he would expose them and glared in unison, so Zhang Heng dissembled his answer and withdrew.

sentences.zh: 閹豎恐終為其患，遂共讒之。

translations.literal: The palace slaves feared he would in the end become their calamity; thereupon together they slandered him.
translations.idiomatic: Those mutilated attendants feared he would ruin them yet and joined in slander.

sentences.zh: 衡常思圖身之事，以為吉凶倚伏，幽微難明，乃作《思玄賦》，[105]以宣寄情志。

translations.literal: Heng constantly pondered matters of securing his person, thinking that good and ill fortune lean on each other and the hidden subtlety is hard to clarify; thereupon he composed the Rhapsody on Pondering the Mystery, [105] in order to declare and lodge his feelings and aims.
translations.idiomatic: Brooding on how to keep himself safe—how blessing and disaster intertwine in ways too subtle to read—he wrote the Rhapsody on Pondering the Mystery [105] to give his heart words.

translations.literal: Its words say:
translations.idiomatic: The piece begins:

sentences.zh: 仰先哲之玄訓兮，雖彌高其弗違。

translations.literal: I look up to the dark instructions of former sages; though ever higher, I do not depart from them.
translations.idiomatic: I lift my eyes to the dark teaching of the ancient sages; however high it rises, I will not stray from it.

sentences.zh: [106]匪仁裡其焉宅兮，匪義多其焉追？

translations.literal: [106] If it is not humaneness, where shall I dwell? If it is not righteousness, what shall I pursue?
translations.idiomatic: [106] Without humaneness, where could I make my home? Without righteousness, what path could I follow?

sentences.zh: [107]潛服膺以永靚兮，綿日月而不衰。

translations.literal: [107] I secretly wear them on the breast and forever keep still; stretching through sun and moon, they do not decay.
translations.idiomatic: [107] I clasp those lessons to my breast in endless quiet; month after month they never fade.

sentences.zh: [108]伊中情之信修兮，慕古人之貞節。

translations.literal: [108] Ah, the good faith and cultivation of my inner heart; I admire the chaste integrity of the ancients.
translations.idiomatic: [108] My heart is tuned to truth; I love the steadfast honor of the old worthies.

sentences.zh: [109]竦余身而順止兮，遵繩墨而不跌。

translations.literal: [109] I straighten my person to follow what is correct; I observe inked line and plumb-line and do not stumble.
translations.idiomatic: [109] I set my body straight on the true path and walk the inked line without a stumble.

sentences.zh: [110]志團團以應懸兮，誠心固其如結。

translations.literal: [110] My will is round as a suspended pearl; my sincere heart is indeed as though tied in a knot.
translations.idiomatic: [110] My will hangs perfect as a suspended pearl; my loyal heart is knotted tight.

sentences.zh: [111]旌性行以制佩兮，佩夜光與瓊枝。

translations.literal: [111] I display nature and conduct to regulate my ornaments; I wear night-glitter and jade branch.
translations.idiomatic: [111] I match my jewels to my nature and bearing, donning gleaming night-gems and jade sprays.

sentences.zh: [112]□幽蘭之秋華兮，又綴之以江蘺。

translations.literal: [112] I pluck the autumn bloom of hidden orchid; I string it together with river Eichhornia.
translations.idiomatic: [112] I gather autumn orchids from hidden vales and twine them with river Eichhornia.

sentences.zh: [113]美襞積以酷裂兮，允塵邈而難虧。

translations.literal: [113] Lovely piled folds split with cruel fragrance; truly the dust is distant and hard to mar.
translations.idiomatic: [113] Rich folds of scent break open in a fierce sweetness, yet the foul world cannot touch them.

sentences.zh: [114]既姱麗而鮮雙兮，非是時之攸珍。

translations.literal: [114] Though lovely and peerless, they are not what this age prizes.
translations.idiomatic: [114] Lovely beyond compare, they are not what this vulgar time esteems.

sentences.zh: [115]奮余榮而莫見兮，播餘香而莫聞。

translations.literal: [115] I wave my glory yet none sees; I scatter my fragrance yet none smells.
translations.idiomatic: [115] I shake out my splendor, yet no eye marks it; I shed my perfume, yet no nose catches it.

sentences.zh: 幽獨守此仄陋兮，敢怠皇而捨勤。

translations.literal: In hidden solitude I keep to this narrow place; I dare not be lazy in majesty or abandon diligence.
translations.idiomatic: Alone in this cramped corner I dare not slacken before Heaven or lay down my toil.

sentences.zh: [116]幸二八之遻虞兮，喜傅說之生殷；

translations.literal: [116] Fortunate are the Eight and the Two who met Shun; I rejoice that Fu Yue rose in Yin;
translations.idiomatic: [116] Happy the Eight and the Two who served Shun; glad am I that Fu Yue was born for Yin;

sentences.zh: 尚前良之遺風兮，恫後辰而無及。

translations.literal: I esteem the lingering breeze of former worthies; I grieve that later days can never catch up.
translations.idiomatic: I honor the last breath of ancient virtue, yet mourn that our age arrives too late to breathe it.

sentences.zh: [117]何孤行之煢煢兮，孑不腢而介立？

translations.literal: [117] Why does my lonely course go so forlorn; alone without a mate I stand apart?
translations.idiomatic: [117] Why must I walk this road alone, companionless, upright amid the crowd?

sentences.zh: 感鸞鷖之特棲兮，悲淑人之稀合。

translations.literal: I am moved that phoenixes roost alone; I grieve that good people rarely find their match.
translations.idiomatic: The phoenix chooses a solitary roost; I grieve that the good seldom meet their like.

translations.literal: [118] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [118] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 彼無合其何傷兮，患觿偽之冒真。

translations.literal: If they do not match me, what harm is there; I fear only that the crowd counterfeits the true.
translations.idiomatic: Their coldness cannot wound me; what I dread is the mob passing off sham for genuine.

sentences.zh: 旦獲讟於腢弟兮，啟金縢而乃信。

translations.literal: At dawn the Duke of Zhou received slander from his brothers; opening the metal coffered bond, then it was believed.
translations.idiomatic: The Duke of Zhou was maligned by kin until the sealed pledge was opened and his loyalty shone clear.

sentences.zh: [119]覽蒸民之多僻兮，畏立辟以危身。

translations.literal: [119] Looking at how depraved the common folk are, I fear to set up laws and endanger my person.
translations.idiomatic: [119] Seeing how warped the world has grown, I shrink from laying down strict law at the cost of my life.

sentences.zh: [120]曾煩毒以迷或兮，羌孰可與言己？

translations.literal: [120] I have been vexed by poison and lost in delusion; who indeed can speak with me of myself?
translations.idiomatic: [120] Worn sick by vexation and half blind with doubt, I find no soul to whom I can open my heart.

sentences.zh: [121]私湛憂而深懷兮，思繽紛而不理。

translations.literal: [121] Privately I sink in sorrow and harbor it deep; my thoughts tangle in confusion and find no order.
translations.idiomatic: [121] In private grief I fold sorrow inward; my thoughts swarm without a thread to sort them.

sentences.zh: [122]願竭力以守義兮，雖貧窮而不改。

translations.literal: [122] I wish to exhaust my strength to keep righteousness; though poor I will not change.
translations.idiomatic: [122] I would spend my last strength for the right, and though destitute I will not bend.

sentences.zh: 執雕虎而試象兮，阽焦原而跟止。

translations.literal: I would grasp the carved tiger and try the elephant; I would stand on the brink of Burnt Plain yet halt my heel.
translations.idiomatic: I would face the carved tiger and trial by elephant, tread the edge of the scorched cliff and still hold my footing.

sentences.zh: [123]庶斯奉以周旋兮，要既死而後已。

translations.literal: [123] I hope to take this as my constant round; the bond runs until death and only then ends.
translations.idiomatic: [123] Let this be the circle I walk to the grave; the vow holds until my last breath.

sentences.zh: [124]俗遷渝而事化兮，泯規矩之圜方。

translations.literal: [124] Custom shifts and slips and affairs transform; the compass and square of circle and square are effaced.
translations.idiomatic: [124] Manners slide and cases change until the plumb line and square no longer mark what is straight.

sentences.zh: [125]珍蕭艾於重笥兮，謂蕙芷之不香。

translations.literal: [125] They treasure mugwort in layered baskets and declare that orchid and iris have no scent.
translations.idiomatic: [125] They pack wormwood in silk-lined cases and swear the orchid has lost its perfume.

sentences.zh: [126]斥西施而弗御兮，羈要褭以服箱。

translations.literal: [126] They reject Xishi and will not mount her; they hobble the fine steed Yaoniao to pull the cart-box.
translations.idiomatic: [126] They spurn the peerless Xi Shi and harness a limping nag to the state coach.

sentences.zh: [127]行陂僻而獲志兮，循法度而離殃。

translations.literal: [127] Crooked ways win their will; following law and measure brings disaster.
translations.idiomatic: [127] The twisted path prospers while the man who keeps the rule walks straight into ruin.

sentences.zh: [128]惟天地之無窮兮，何遭遇之無常！

translations.literal: [128] Only heaven and earth know no end; how unconstant are the meetings of fate!
translations.idiomatic: [128] Heaven and earth alone know no bound; human fortune shifts without warning.

sentences.zh: 不抑操而苟容兮，譬臨河而無航。

translations.literal: If I do not bend my principles to curry favor, it is like coming to a river without a boat.
translations.idiomatic: Refuse to crook my integrity for favor, and it is like standing on a bank with no ferry.

sentences.zh: [129]欲巧笑以干媚兮，非余心之所嘗。

translations.literal: [129] To want clever smiles to beg for charm is not what my heart has ever tasted.
translations.idiomatic: [129] To trade pretty grins for patronage is a flavor my heart has never craved.

sentences.zh: 襲溫恭之黻衣兮，披禮義之繡裳。

translations.literal: I put on the patterned robe of mild reverence; I spread the embroidered skirt of ritual and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: I don the dark robe of gentle reverence and wrap myself in the brocade of rite and right.

sentences.zh: [130]辮貞亮以為鞶兮，雜技蓺以為珩。

translations.literal: [130] I braid steadfast brightness to make my belt-ornament; I mix many arts to make my pendant-stone.
translations.idiomatic: [130] I weave constancy into the jade at my belt and thread every useful art into the stone at my side.

sentences.zh: [131]昭彩藻與雕琢兮，璜聲遠而彌長。

translations.literal: [131] I display bright pattern and carving; the huang-gem's sound travels far and grows ever longer.
translations.idiomatic: [131] Bright colors and careful carving ring from my huang-pendant, its note carrying far.

sentences.zh: [132]淹棲□以恣欲兮，耀靈忽其西藏。

translations.literal: [132] I long linger in idleness to indulge desire; the dazzling spirit suddenly sets in the west.
translations.idiomatic: [132] I dawdle in ease feeding appetite while the sun plunges toward the western dark.

sentences.zh: [133]恃己知而華予兮，鶗□鳴而不芳。

translations.literal: [133] Relying on private knowledge they flatter me; the shrike cries yet nothing is fragrant.
translations.idiomatic: [133] They praise me to my face with private conceit, yet the shrike's cry brings no perfume.

sentences.zh: [134]冀一年之三秀兮，遒白露之為霜。

translations.literal: [134] I hoped the plant might flower thrice in one year; white dew hastens and turns to frost.
translations.idiomatic: [134] I hoped for three blooms in a single season, but autumn frost follows hard on the dew.

sentences.zh: [135]時亹亹而代序兮，疇可與乎比伉？

translations.literal: [135] The seasons march in endless sequence; who can be my peer and mate?
translations.idiomatic: [135] Time flows on in ordered steps; who under heaven can walk beside me as equal?

sentences.zh: [136]咨妒嫮之難並兮，想依韓以流亡，[137]恐漸冉而無成兮，留則蔽而不章。

translations.literal: [136] I sigh that jealous beauty is hard to join; I think to cling to Han and wander in exile, [137] yet I fear dragging on without achievement; to stay is to be hidden and not shine.
translations.idiomatic: [136] Jealous rivals will not share one throne; I think of fleeing to Han for refuge, [137] yet dread years of drift without a name, and dread obscurity should I remain.

sentences.zh: 心猶與而狐疑兮，即岐址而攄情。

translations.literal: My heart still hesitates in fox-like doubt; I arrive at the foot of Qi and pour out my feeling.
translations.idiomatic: My heart wavers like a fox between burrows; I come to Qi's slopes and lay bare my doubt.

sentences.zh: [138]文君為我端蓍兮，利飛遁以保名。

translations.literal: [138] King Wen sets the milfoil straight for me; it favors swift withdrawal to preserve my name.
translations.idiomatic: [138] King Wen reads the stalks for me; the omen favors swift flight to keep my good name.

sentences.zh: [139]歷觿山以周流兮，翼迅風以揚聲。

translations.literal: [139] I pass all mountains in wandering; swift wind lifts my wings and carries my cry.
translations.idiomatic: [139] I wheel past every peak; the gale lifts my wings and bears my cry abroad.

sentences.zh: [140]二女感於崇岳兮，或冰折而不營。

translations.literal: [140] The two women were moved on the high peak; some broke like ice and would not build.
translations.idiomatic: [140] The two consorts answered the sacred height; some, like cracking ice, would not complete the work.

sentences.zh: [141]天蓋高而為澤兮，誰云路之不平！

translations.literal: [141] The canopy is high and becomes marsh; who says the road is not level!
translations.idiomatic: [141] Heaven spreads wide as a marsh above; who dares call the way uneven?

sentences.zh: [142]□自強而不息兮，蹈玉階之嶢崢。

translations.literal: [142] I strive without ceasing; I tread the jade steps' steep height.
translations.idiomatic: [142] I press upward without rest and climb the steep stair of jade.

sentences.zh: [143]懼筮氏之長短兮，鑽東□以觀禎。

translations.literal: [143] I fear the diviner's long and short judgments; I bore eastward to read the omens.
translations.idiomatic: [143] I dread the torturer's tricks with the stalks and bore eastward seeking a clear sign.

sentences.zh: [144]遇九□之介鳥兮，怨素意之不逞。

translations.literal: [144] I met the great bird of the nine wilds; I resent that plain sincerity cannot prevail.
translations.idiomatic: [144] I met the giant bird of the nine wastes; my honest heart still wins no hearing.

sentences.zh: [145]游塵外而瞥天兮，據冥翳而哀鳴。

translations.literal: [145] I roam beyond the dust and glimpse heaven; I perch in dark shade and cry in sorrow.
translations.idiomatic: [145] I drift beyond the red dust to glimpse the sky, then roost in shadow and wail my grief.

sentences.zh: [146]鵰鶚競於貪婪兮，我修絜以益榮。

translations.literal: [146] Kites and ospreys vie in greed; I cultivate purity and grow more honored.
translations.idiomatic: [146] While hawks quarrel for carrion, I polish purity and my honor only grows.

sentences.zh: [147]子有故於玄鳥兮，歸母氏而後寧。

translations.literal: [147] You, dark bird, have old ties with me; return to your mother's house and afterward be at peace.
translations.idiomatic: [147] Swallow, you and I share an ancient bond; go home to the nest of your kin and find peace.

translations.literal: [148] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [148] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 占既吉而無悔兮，簡元辰而俶裝。

translations.literal: The divination was auspicious without regret; I chose the primal day and began packing.
translations.idiomatic: The oracle came back lucky and clear; I chose the first good day and made ready to travel.

sentences.zh: [149]旦余沐於清原兮，晞余發於朝陽。

translations.literal: [149] At dawn I bathed in the Clear Source; I dried my hair in the morning sun.
translations.idiomatic: [149] At dawn I washed in the Clear Source and combed my hair dry in the rising sun.

sentences.zh: [150]漱飛泉之瀝液兮，咀石菌之流英。

translations.literal: [150] I rinsed my mouth with the flying spring's dripping liquor; I chewed the stone fungus' flowing blossom.
translations.idiomatic: [150] I gargled with jetting spring-water and chewed the gleaming spores of cliff fungus.

sentences.zh: [151]翾鳥舉而魚躍兮，將往走乎八荒。

translations.literal: [151] Whirling birds rose and fish leaped; I was about to run to the eight wilds.
translations.idiomatic: [151] Birds shot upward and fish leapt from the pool; I set my face toward the ends of the earth.

sentences.zh: [152]過少皞之窮野兮，問三丘乎句芒。

translations.literal: [152] I passed Shaohao's barren wild and asked the three mounds of Gou Mang.
translations.idiomatic: [152] I crossed Shaohao's waste and questioned Gou Mang among the three sacred peaks.

sentences.zh: [153]何道真之淳粹兮，去穢累而票輕。

translations.literal: [153] How pure and perfect was the true Way; casting off filth I floated light.
translations.idiomatic: [153] How flawless the true Way; I shook off the world's mire and felt my body grow light.

sentences.zh: [154]登蓬萊而容與兮，鰲雖抃而不傾。

translations.literal: [154] I ascended Penglai and lingered; though the leviathan slapped the waves, it did not capsize.
translations.idiomatic: [154] I climbed Penglai and dallied there; the sea-monster thrashed, yet the isle never tilted.

sentences.zh: [155]留瀛洲而采芝兮，聊且以乎長生。

translations.literal: [155] I stayed on Yingzhou gathering fungus; for the nonce I sought long life thereby.
translations.idiomatic: [155] I lingered on Yingzhou plucking magic herbs, hoping thereby to cheat time awhile.

sentences.zh: [156]憑歸雲而遐逝兮，夕余宿乎扶桑。

translations.literal: [156] Riding homeward clouds I sped far off; at evening I lodged at Fusang.
translations.idiomatic: [156] I rode the sunset clouds into distance and made my camp that night beneath Fusang.

sentences.zh: [157]腔青岑之玉醴兮，餐沆瀣以為糧。
