translations.literal: [157] I sipped jade nectar from Green Hill; I took night-dew for grain.
translations.idiomatic: [157] I drank the jade liquor of Green Hill and supped on night-dew for my rice.

sentences.zh: [158]發昔夢於木禾兮，谷櫫□之高岡。

translations.literal: [158] I dreamed of old of the tree of grain; in the valley of high ridges I gathered its fruit.
translations.idiomatic: [158] A former dream showed me the tree of grain; I climbed a towering ridge to gather its fruit.

sentences.zh: [159]朝吾行於湯谷兮，從伯禹於稽山。

translations.literal: [159] In the morning I went to the Valley of Hot Waters; I followed the Earl Yu at Mount Ji.
translations.idiomatic: [159] At dawn I reached the Valley of Dawn and walked with Yu the Great on Mount Ji.

sentences.zh: [160]集腢神之執玉兮，疾防風之食言。

translations.literal: [160] I gathered the gods holding jade; I hastened to punish Fangfeng for breaking his word.
translations.idiomatic: [160] I watched the spirits bearing jade tablets and hurried to punish Fangfeng the oath-breaker.

translations.literal: [161] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [161] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 指長沙以邪徑兮，存重華乎南鄰。

translations.literal: I point to Changsha by a winding path; I visit Chonghua as my southern neighbor.
translations.idiomatic: I steer toward Changsha by a crooked trail and call on Shun as my neighbor to the south.

sentences.zh: [162]哀二妃之未從兮，翩儐處彼湘瀕。

translations.literal: [162] I mourn that the two consorts did not follow; lightly they dwell on the Xiang shore.
translations.idiomatic: [162] I grieve that Ehuang and Nüying never followed their lord, but flutter alone on the Xiang bank.

sentences.zh: [163]流目眺夫衡阿兮，睹有黎之圮墳；

translations.literal: [163] I let my eyes roam Mount Heng slope and saw the ruined tombs of Youli;
translations.idiomatic: [163] I swept my gaze along Mount Heng and saw the crumbled mounds of Youli;

sentences.zh: 痛火正之無懷兮，托山陂以孤魂。

translations.literal: I grieved that Huozheng had no posterity; I entrusted a lone soul to the hillside.
translations.idiomatic: I mourned Zhurong line cut short and lodged a wandering ghost on the hill.

sentences.zh: [164]愁蔚蔚以慕遠兮，越卬州而愉敖。

translations.literal: [164] Melancholy thick, I longed for the far distance; I crossed Yingzhou and roamed in idle joy.
translations.idiomatic: [164] Heavy with sorrow, I yearned for distant shores, crossed Yingzhou, and wandered in brief ease.

sentences.zh: [165]躋日中於昆吾兮，憩炎天之所陶。

translations.literal: [165] I climbed Kunwu at midday; I rested where the blazing sky was fired in a kiln.
translations.idiomatic: [165] At noon I scaled Kunwu and rested where the furnace of heaven scorches the earth.

sentences.zh: [166]揚芒熛而絳天兮，水泫沄而湧濤。

translations.literal: [166] Flaming banners dyed the sky crimson; the waters swirled in surging billows.
translations.idiomatic: [166] Banners of flame stained the heavens red while the rivers boiled into surf.

sentences.zh: [167]溫風翕其增熱兮，惄鬱邑其難聊。

translations.literal: [167] Warm winds gathered and added heat; vexation and depression were hard to endure.
translations.idiomatic: [167] Hot winds piled heat upon heat until sullen gloom became unbearable.

sentences.zh: [168]顝羈旅而無友兮，余安能乎留茲？

translations.literal: [168] Wandering in misery without friends; how could I stay here long?
translations.idiomatic: [168] A stranger in misery, friendless—how could I linger in such a place?

translations.literal: [169] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [169] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 顧金天而歎息兮，吾欲往乎西嬉。

translations.literal: I turned toward Metal Heaven and sighed; I wished to go west for sport.
translations.idiomatic: I glanced toward the western sky and sighed, longing to roam in the western wastes.

sentences.zh: [170]前祝融使舉麾兮，纚朱鳥以承旗。

translations.literal: [170] I sent Zhurong ahead to raise the banner; I trailed the vermilion bird to bear the flag.
translations.idiomatic: [170] I bade Zhurong lift the standard before me and let the red bird trail my flag.

sentences.zh: [171]躔建木於廣都兮，拓若華而躊躇。

translations.literal: [171] I trod the Cosmic Tree at Guangdu; I plucked Ruohua bloom and hesitated.
translations.idiomatic: [171] I trod the Cosmic Tree in the broad capital and plucked the flower of Ruohua, pausing in doubt.

sentences.zh: [172]超軒轅於西海兮，跨汪氏之龍魚；

translations.literal: [172] I leaped beyond Xuanyuan in the western sea; I straddled Wangshi dragon-fish;
translations.idiomatic: [172] I vaulted past Xuanyuan on the western ocean and rode Wangshi dragon-fish;

sentences.zh: 聞此國之千歲兮，曾焉足以娛余？

translations.literal: I heard this land boasts a thousand years; how could that ever amuse me?
translations.idiomatic: They say this realm lives a thousand years—what pleasure could that bring me?

translations.literal: [173] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [173] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 思九土之殊風兮，從蓐收而遂徂。

translations.literal: I pondered the nine soils different winds; I followed Rushou and went on.
translations.idiomatic: I pondered the nine regions strange airs and followed Rushou ever westward.

sentences.zh: [174]欻神化而蟬蛻兮，朋精粹而為徒。

translations.literal: [174] Suddenly spirit-transformation like a cicada molt; I took pure essence as my companion.
translations.idiomatic: [174] In a flash the spirit sloughed its shell; I made distilled essence my only mate.

sentences.zh: [175]蹶白門而東馳兮，雲台行乎中野。

translations.literal: [175] I sped through White Gate eastward; Cloud Terrace marched across the mid-plain.
translations.idiomatic: [175] I galloped east from White Gate while Cloud Terrace rolled across the central wilds.

sentences.zh: [176]亂弱水之潺湲兮，逗華陰之湍渚。

translations.literal: [176] I leapt the weak water murmuring flow; I halted at the rushing shallows of Huayin.
translations.idiomatic: [176] I cleared the whispering Weak Water and rested on Huayin racing shoals.

sentences.zh: [177]號馮夷俾清津兮，棹龍舟以濟予。

translations.literal: [177] I called to Pingyi to clear the ford; I plied the dragon boat to ferry me.
translations.idiomatic: [177] I summoned River Lord Pingyi to calm the ford and poled a dragon boat across.

sentences.zh: [178]會帝軒之未歸兮，悵相佯而延佇。

translations.literal: [178] The Emperor of the Blues had not yet returned; I lingered, pacing in long waiting.
translations.idiomatic: [178] The Blue Emperor was still away; I paced the bank in restless delay.

sentences.zh: [179]呬河林之蓁蓁兮，偉關睢之戒女。

translations.literal: [179] I sighed at the river grove luxuriance; I marveled at the Guanju warning to girls.
translations.idiomatic: [179] I marveled at the river-bank thick growth and the lesson of Guanju for young wives.

sentences.zh: [180]黃靈詹而訪命兮，摎天道其焉如。

translations.literal: [180] The Yellow Spirit appeared and I asked my fate; I traced Heaven way to learn its bent.
translations.idiomatic: [180] The Yellow Spirit came down; I begged to know my lot and traced Heaven road to see where it turns.

sentences.zh: [181]曰近信而遠疑兮，六籍闕而不書。

translations.literal: [181] It said: trust the near and doubt the far; the six classics leave such things unwritten.
translations.idiomatic: [181] The voice said: trust what is near, doubt what is remote—the Six Classics never wrote of this.

sentences.zh: [182]神逵昧其難覆兮，疇克謨而從諸？

translations.literal: [182] The divine path lies dark and hard to cover; who can plan it and follow?
translations.idiomatic: [182] The god-road lies veiled beyond tracing; who can map it and keep the course?

sentences.zh: [183]牛哀病而成虎兮，雖逢昆其必噬。

translations.literal: [183] Niu Ai sickened and became a tiger; though he met his own brother he was sure to devour him.
translations.idiomatic: [183] Niu Ai fell ill into tiger shape; even meeting kin he could not stay his jaws.

sentences.zh: [184]□令殪而屍亡兮，取蜀禪而引世。

translations.literal: [184] His order perished and his corpse vanished; he seized Shu succession and dragged out generations.
translations.idiomatic: [184] His mandate died and his body vanished, yet he seized the succession of Shu and stretched his line across ages.

sentences.zh: [185]死生錯而不齊兮，雖司命其不晰。

translations.literal: [185] Death and life cross and do not align; though the Director of Fates rules, it is not clear.
translations.idiomatic: [185] Life and death tangle without pattern; though Fate holds the tally, its meaning stays dark.

sentences.zh: [186]竇號行於代路兮，後膺祚而繁廡。

translations.literal: [186] Dou rose along the byway of usurpation; later she received the throne and multiplied her halls.
translations.idiomatic: [186] Dou climbed by a crooked path, then won the mandate and piled palace upon palace.

sentences.zh: [187]王肆侈於漢庭兮，卒銜恤而絕緒。

translations.literal: [187] Wang Mang wantoned in the Han court; in the end he met grief and his line was cut.
translations.idiomatic: [187] Wang Mang rioted in the Han halls and ended in mourning with his line snapped.

sentences.zh: [188]尉尨眉而郎潛兮，逮三葉而遘武。

translations.literal: [188] Yan was a clerk with shaggy brows yet hid as a gentleman; after three generations he met Emperor Wu.
translations.idiomatic: [188] Yan, bushy-browed clerk, posed as a courtier and, three reigns later, won audience with Han Wudi.

sentences.zh: [189]董弱冠而司袞兮，設王隧而弗處。

translations.literal: [189] Dong, in his capping year, held the minister robe; he laid out the king tunnel yet did not occupy it.
translations.idiomatic: [189] Dong Zhongshu, barely capped, took the minister silks, planned the imperial tunnel, yet never walked it.

sentences.zh: [190]夫吉凶之相仍兮，恆反側而靡所。

translations.literal: [190] Good and ill fortune follow one another in turn; ever they reverse and leave no fixed place.
translations.idiomatic: [190] Blessing and curse chase each other in circles; they wheel and reverse without a resting point.

sentences.zh: 穆負天以悅牛兮，豎亂叔而幽主。

translations.literal: Mu won heaven favor with a cattle dealer; the boy brought chaos to his uncle and imprisoned his lord.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Mu bought Heaven smile with cattle; a boy eunuch sowed strife among uncles and caged his king.

sentences.zh: [191]文斷袪而忌伯兮，閹謁賊而寧後。

translations.literal: [191] Wen cut his sleeve in fear of Bo; the eunuch slandered yet the queen was spared.
translations.idiomatic: [191] Jin Wen cut his sleeve to flee Bo; a eunuch slander yet left the heir safe.

sentences.zh: [192]通人闇於好惡兮，豈愛惑之能剖？

translations.literal: [192] The enlightened are blinded in love and hate; how can fond delusion be dissected?
translations.idiomatic: [192] Even the wise grow blind to love and hate; who can cut through such bewitchment?

sentences.zh: [193]嬴擿讖而戒胡兮，備諸外而發內。

translations.literal: [193] Ying Zheng plucked a prophecy warning of the Hu; he guarded against outsiders while disaster rose within.
translations.idiomatic: [193] The First Emperor seized a weft-text about the Hu, guarded the frontier, and missed the peril within.

sentences.zh: [194]或輦賄而違車兮，孕行產而為對。

translations.literal: [194] Some hauled bribes and missed the chariot; a woman walked and bore a mate.
translations.idiomatic: [194] Some loaded treasure yet missed the royal coach; a woman walked abroad and brought back a consort.

sentences.zh: [195]慎醋顯於言天兮，占水火而妄誶。

translations.literal: [195] The prognosticator spoke clearly of heaven; he divined water and fire yet raved falsely.
translations.idiomatic: [195] The seer sounded wise on Heaven yet babbled nonsense when he cast yarrow for water and fire.

sentences.zh: [196]梁叟患夫黎丘兮，丁厥子而事刃，親所睇而弗識兮，矧幽冥之可信。

translations.literal: [196] The old man of Liang feared the demon of Li Hill; he struck his true son with a blade; what the eye saw he did not recognize; how much less the dark unseen.
translations.idiomatic: [196] The graybeard of Liang dreaded Li Hill ghost, slew his own son, mistook kin for fiend—if daylight deceives, what trust lies in the dark?

sentences.zh: [197]毋綿攣以涬己兮，思百憂以自疢。

translations.literal: [197] Do not bind yourself in floating license; brooding a hundred griefs will sicken you.
translations.idiomatic: [197] Do not tie yourself to reckless drift; nursing a hundred sorrows will waste the flesh.

sentences.zh: [198]彼天監之孔明兮，用棐忱而佑仁。

translations.literal: [198] That heaven oversight is supremely bright; it uses sincere trust to aid the humane.
translations.idiomatic: [198] Heaven gaze is clearest of all; it aids the humane through steadfast faith.

sentences.zh: [199]湯蠲體以禱祈兮，蒙厖禠以拯人。

translations.literal: [199] Tang offered his body in prayer; he received great blessing to save the people.
translations.idiomatic: [199] Tang offered his own body in sacrifice and won a mighty boon to ransom the people.

sentences.zh: [200]景三慮以營國兮，熒惑次於它辰。

translations.literal: [200] Jing thrice pondered to order the state; Mars lodged in another mansion.
translations.idiomatic: [200] Duke Jing of Jin thrice planned the realm, yet Mars shifted into an alien house.

sentences.zh: [201]魏顆亮以從理兮，鬼亢回以敝秦。

translations.literal: [201] Wei Ke kept faith with reason; ghosts turned the battle and broke Qin.
translations.idiomatic: [201] Wei Ke honored a dead father wish; ghosts bent the lines and shattered Qin.

sentences.zh: [202]咎繇邁而種德兮，德樹茂乎英、六。

translations.literal: [202] Gao Yao walked on and planted virtue; his virtue flourished in Ying and Liu.
translations.idiomatic: [202] Gao Yao strode onward sowing virtue until his merit bloomed in Ying and Liu.

sentences.zh: [203]桑末寄夫根生兮，卉既雕而已毓。

translations.literal: [203] Though the mulberry tip is grafted on another root, the plant withers yet still bears seed.
translations.idiomatic: [203] A mulberry slip grafted to a foreign stock may look dead, yet life stirs again beneath the bark.

sentences.zh: [204]有無言而不讎兮，又何往而不復？

translations.literal: [204] Is there speech that finds no answering echo; where would I go and not return?
translations.idiomatic: [204] What word has ever been spoken that Heaven does not answer? What journey ends without a road home?

sentences.zh: [205]盍遠多以飛聲兮，孰謂時之可蓄？

translations.literal: [205] Why not range far and let your fame fly—who says the moment can be hoarded?
translations.idiomatic: [205] Better to ride far and let your name ring out than to pretend time can be bottled.

translations.literal: [206] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [206] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 仰矯首以遙望兮，魂□惘而無疇。

translations.literal: I lift my head and gaze into the distance; my soul is dazed and finds no target.
translations.idiomatic: I crane my neck toward the horizon, yet my spirit wanders blind, with nowhere to fix its gaze.

sentences.zh: [207]偪區中之隘陋兮，將北度而宣游。

translations.literal: [207] The central region's narrowness presses me; I will cross northward and wander in proclamation.
translations.idiomatic: [207] The cramped heartland stifles me; I mean to cross the north and roam in freedom.

sentences.zh: [208]行積冰之磑磑兮，清泉冱而不流。

translations.literal: [208] I travel over piled ice, grinding and gleaming; clear springs freeze and cease to flow.
translations.idiomatic: [208] I cross fields of blue ice that grind underfoot; even clear springs lock shut and will not run.

sentences.zh: [209]寒風淒而永至兮，拂穹岫之騷騷。

translations.literal: [209] Cold wind blows, endlessly arriving; it brushes the vault of peaks with a restless sigh.
translations.idiomatic: [209] The north wind never rests; it scrapes the mountain sky with a long, keening note.

sentences.zh: 玄武縮於殼中兮，螣蛇蜿而自糾。

translations.literal: The Dark Warrior shrinks inside his shell; the flying serpent winds and coils on itself.
translations.idiomatic: The tortoise god curls tight in his carapace; the winged serpent knots itself in frozen spirals.

sentences.zh: [210]魚矜鱗而□凌兮，鳥登木而失條。

translations.literal: [210] Fish purse their scales and leap the ice; birds mount the trees and lose their perches.
translations.idiomatic: [210] Fish arch their scales to vault the ice; birds flutter to bare branches that will not hold.

sentences.zh: [211]坐太陰之屏室兮，慨含欷而增愁。

translations.literal: [211] I sit in the Great Yin's curtained chamber; I sigh with stifled sobs and grief increases.
translations.idiomatic: [211] I sit in the moon's shadowed hall and choke on sobs as sorrow swells.

sentences.zh: [212]怨高陽之相寓兮，□顓頊之宅幽。

translations.literal: [212] I resent Gaoyang's lodging here; I pity Zhuanxu's dwelling in the dark.
translations.idiomatic: [212] I rail that Gaoyang should house himself here, and pity Zhuanxu for his exile in the gloom.

sentences.zh: [213]庸織絡於四裔兮，斯與彼其何瘳？

translations.literal: [213] To weave cords stretching to the four frontiers—what cure can that bring them or me?
translations.idiomatic: [213] What healing comes of spinning threads to the world's four edges?

sentences.zh: [214]望寒門之絕垠兮，縱余增乎不周。

translations.literal: [214] I gaze on Cold Gate's sheer edge; I give my rein free rein toward Mount Buzhou.
translations.idiomatic: [214] I stare past the Gate of Cold to the world's rim and slack the rein toward perilous Buzhou.

sentences.zh: [215]迅蹣潚其媵我兮，騖翩躑而不禁。

translations.literal: [215] Swift whirlwinds escort me as bridesmaids; winged sprites flutter and cannot be restrained.
translations.idiomatic: [215] Gale spirits race as my handmaids; winged fairies wheel wild and will not be checked.

sentences.zh: [216]趨谽□之洞穴兮，摽通淵之碄碄。

translations.literal: [216] I hurry to the yawning cavern's mouth; I strike the deep abyss's jagged teeth.
translations.idiomatic: [216] I rush the gulf's black maw and beat the fanged cliffs of the sunken pit.

sentences.zh: [217]經重陰乎寂寞兮，愍墳羊之潛深。

translations.literal: [217] I pass through layered yin in utter silence; I pity the rams that lurk in the deep.
translations.idiomatic: [217] I thread double darkness, utterly still, and mourn the tomb-rams hidden in the depths.

translations.literal: [218] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [218] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 追慌忽於地底兮，軼無形而上浮。

translations.literal: I chase blurried shadows in the earth's bowels; I leap past formless things and float upward.
translations.idiomatic: I pursue dim shapes through the underworld, then burst past bodiless shades toward the upper air.

sentences.zh: [219]出右密之闇野兮，不識蹊之所由。

translations.literal: [219] I emerge into Youmi's dark wilds; I cannot tell which path the trail should take.
translations.idiomatic: [219] I break out into Youmi's sunless waste and lose every trace of a human track.

sentences.zh: [220]速燭龍令執炬兮，過鐘山而中休。

translations.literal: [220] I speed Zhulong to grasp the torch; I pass Mount Bell and rest midway.
translations.idiomatic: [220] I bid Torch Dragon seize the flame and halt midway at somber Mount Bell.

sentences.zh: [221]瞰瑤溪之赤岸兮，吊祖江之見劉。

translations.literal: [221] I look down on Jasper Brook's red cliffs; I mourn Zujiang, murdered by Liu.
translations.idiomatic: [221] I peer over crimson banks of Jasper Brook and weep for Zujiang, struck down by Liu.

sentences.zh: [222]聘王母於銀臺兮，羞玉芝以療饑；

translations.literal: [222] I visit the Queen Mother on her silver terrace; I offer jade fungus to cure hunger;
translations.idiomatic: [222] I call on the Queen Mother on her silver tower and bring jade mushrooms to stay my hunger;

sentences.zh: [223]戴勝憖其既歡兮，又誚余之行□。

translations.literal: [223] Dai Sheng frowns, already pleased; she also mocks my wandering course.
translations.idiomatic: [223] Xi Wangmu's crown-maid frowns, half pleased, and jeers at my restless journey.

sentences.zh: [224]載太華之玉女兮，召洛浦之宓妃。

translations.literal: [224] I mount the jade maid of Mount Taihua; I summon Luofu's Fufei.
translations.idiomatic: [224] I take the Taihua maiden for companion and call Fufei from the Luo's shore.

sentences.zh: [225]咸姣麗以蠱媚兮，增嫮眼而蛾眉。

translations.literal: [225] Both are lovely, using bewitching charm; they widen lovely eyes and moth eyebrows.
translations.idiomatic: [225] Both are ravishing, skilled in sorcerous grace; their bright eyes widen, their moth brows arch.

sentences.zh: [226]舒妙婧之纖□兮，揚雜錯之簹徽。

translations.literal: [226] They spread slender beauty's gauzy train; they raise mixed patterns on bamboo flutes.
translations.idiomatic: [226] They trail filmy sleeves of delicate grace and lift wild motifs on pierced bamboo flutes.

sentences.zh: [227]離朱唇而微笑兮，顏的□以遺光。

translations.literal: [227] They part vermilion lips in faint smiles; their faces' dimples cast stray light.
translations.idiomatic: [227] They part carmine lips in quiet smiles; dimples flash like stray sunbeams.

sentences.zh: [228]獻環琨與璵縭兮，申厥好以玄黃。

translations.literal: [228] They present ring-jade, fine jade, and silken cords; they renew their favor with dark and yellow silks.
translations.idiomatic: [228] They offer rings, disks, and twisted silk cords, pledging love with bolts of black and gold.

sentences.zh: [229]雖色艷而賂美兮，志浩蕩而不嘉。

translations.literal: [229] Though their colors are bright and their bribes lovely, my will is vast and does not approve.
translations.idiomatic: [229] However bright their paint, however rich their gifts, my heart stays wide and cold to the bargain.

sentences.zh: [230]雙材悲於不納兮，並詠詩而清歌。

translations.literal: [230] The twin beauties grieve at not being accepted; together they chant poems in clear song.
translations.idiomatic: [230] Both beauties weep at my refusal, then join their voices in a pure, measured song.

translations.literal: [231] The song says: Heaven and earth steam and fuse; the hundred plants hold their blossoms.
translations.idiomatic: [231] Their song runs: Heaven and earth breathe together; every plant cups a flower.

translations.literal: Calling cranes entwine their necks; the ospreys answer one another.
translations.idiomatic: Cranes cry neck to neck; fish-hawks call and answer across the water.

translations.literal: The maiden harbors spring; her essence and soul reel and shift.
translations.idiomatic: The girl feels spring's ache; her very soul shivers and turns.

translations.literal: [232] How can you, clear-sighted one, forget me so utterly?
translations.idiomatic: [232] O clear-eyed lord, how could you forget me as deeply as this?

translations.literal: [233] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [233] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 將荅賦而不暇兮，爰整駕而亟行。

translations.literal: I would answer their rhapsody but have no leisure; therefore I array my chariot and hurry on.
translations.idiomatic: I have no time to answer their song in kind, so I hitch the team and press the road.

sentences.zh: [234]瞻櫫□之巍巍兮，臨縈河之洋洋。

translations.literal: [234] I look up at the towering quince tree; I descend on the vast, coiling Yellow River.
translations.idiomatic: [234] I lift my eyes to a towering quince and drop toward the Yellow River's endless coil.

sentences.zh: 伏靈□以負坻兮，□螭龍之飛梁。

translations.literal: I couch the spirit tortoise to bear the islet; I span the hornless dragons as a flying bridge.
translations.idiomatic: I bid the divine tortoise shoulder the shoal and string hornless dragons for a sky-bridge.

sentences.zh: [235]登閬風之曾城兮，構不死而為默。

translations.literal: [235] I climb Langfeng's tiered citadel; I pile never-dying herbs into a silent store.
translations.idiomatic: [235] I scale Langfeng's stacked towers and heap immortal herbs into a soundless hoard.

sentences.zh: [236]屑瑤繠以為炮兮，□白水以為漿。

translations.literal: [236] I crumble jade meal for provisions; I draw white water for drink.
translations.idiomatic: [236] I pound jade dust for rations and draw snow-bright water for wine.

sentences.zh: [237]抨巫咸以占夢兮，乃貞吉之元符。

translations.literal: [237] I summon Wu Xian to interpret the dream; it is the primal omen of steadfast good fortune.
translations.idiomatic: [237] I call Wu Xian to read the dream, and the stalks show the root-sign of lasting luck.

translations.literal: [238] I nurture commanding virtue in the central balance;
translations.idiomatic: [238] I feed true virtue in the heart's still center;

translations.literal: (join)
translations.idiomatic: (join)

translations.literal: *bearing fine* (variant graph in the received text)
translations.idiomatic: bearing the fine inward fragrance

translations.literal: (grain)
translations.idiomatic: (grain)

translations.literal: *grain ears* to spread abroad (variant graph in the received text).
translations.idiomatic: letting the full ear scatter its seed abroad.

sentences.zh: [239]既垂穎而顧本兮，爾要思乎故居。

translations.literal: [239] Having hung down ears, I look back to the root; you must think of your old home.
translations.idiomatic: [239] The ripe ear bows toward its stalk; so you must remember the soil that bore you.

sentences.zh: [240]安和靜而隨時兮，姑純懿之所廬。

translations.literal: [240] Keep peace and quiet and follow the times; for the nonce take pure excellence as your hut.
translations.idiomatic: [240] Stay calm, move with the hour, and for now let flawless goodness be your only roof.

translations.literal: [241] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [241] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 戒庶寮以夙會兮，僉恭職而並迓。

translations.literal: I warn the host of officers to assemble at dawn; all reverently take their posts and wait together.
translations.idiomatic: I bid every minister meet at cockcrow; each takes his station and waits in solemn ranks.

sentences.zh: [242]豐隆軯其震霆兮，列缺曄其照夜。

translations.literal: [242] Fenglong rolls his thunder; Lieque flashes, lighting the night.
translations.idiomatic: [242] Lord Thunder rolls his peal; the lightning spirit rips the dark with white fire.

sentences.zh: [243]雲師□以交集兮，涷雨沛其灑塗。

translations.literal: [243] The cloud master gathers hosts; freezing rain pours, splashing the road.
translations.idiomatic: [243] The cloud marshal stacks the squalls; sleet hammers down and sluices the dust.

sentences.zh: [244]轙琱輿而樹葩兮，擾應龍以服輅。

translations.literal: [244] I carve the jade carriage and set up blossoms; I harness the responding dragon to draw the hearse.
translations.idiomatic: [244] I chase the jade coach with carved flowers and hitch the answering dragon to the shaft.

sentences.zh: [245]百神森其備從兮，屯騎羅而星布。

translations.literal: [245] The hundred gods stand thick as escort; squadrons of horse array like scattered stars.
translations.idiomatic: [245] A hundred deities mass as outriders; chariot squadrons net the sky like stars.

sentences.zh: [246]振余袂而就車兮，修□揭以低昂。

translations.literal: [246] I shake my sleeves and mount the car; long pennants dip and sway, high and low.
translations.idiomatic: [246] I flap my sleeves and spring to the car; long banners dip and toss in the wind.

sentences.zh: [247]冠咢咢其映蓋兮，佩綝纚以輝煌。

translations.literal: [247] The tall crown flashes against the canopy; pendants tinkle, blazing bright.
translations.idiomatic: [247] My tall crown flashes under the awning; girdle-gems chime in dazzling light.

sentences.zh: [248]僕夫儼其正策兮，八乘攄而超驤。

translations.literal: [248] The groomsmen stand straight with tightened reins; eight teams surge forward, racing in flight.
translations.idiomatic: [248] Grooms brace the traces; eight teams of horses leap as one.

sentences.zh: [249]氛旄溶以天旋兮，蜺旌飄而飛揚。

translations.literal: [249] Mist banners melt and wheel with heaven; rainbow flags stream and fly.
translations.idiomatic: [249] Vapor standards melt into the turning sky; iridescent pennants stream on the gale.

sentences.zh: [250]撫軨軹而還睨兮，心灼藥其如湯。

translations.literal: [250] I grasp the axle-cap and look back; my heart burns like boiling broth.
translations.idiomatic: [250] I clutch the hub-cap and glance behind; my breast boils like a cauldron.

sentences.zh: [251]羨上都之赫戲兮，何迷故而不忘？

translations.literal: [251] I envy the upper capital's splendor—why am I spellbound by what I left?
translations.idiomatic: [251] I marvel at the high city's blaze—why should the old haunt still bind me?

sentences.zh: [252]左青琱以揵芝兮，右素威以司鉦。

translations.literal: [252] At left the green dragon holds the fungus; at right Baihu strikes the gong.
translations.idiomatic: [252] The azure dragon on the left bears magic moss; the White Tiger on the right beats bronze.

sentences.zh: [253]前長離使拂羽兮，委水衡乎玄冥。

translations.literal: [253] Before, Changli brushes his plumes; behind, the water marshal waits on Dark North.
translations.idiomatic: [253] The vermilion bird fluffs his wings ahead; astern the river officer serves Dark North.

sentences.zh: [254]屬箕伯以函風兮，征淟涊而為清。

translations.literal: [254] I charge Lord Basket to box the wind; I summon the murky vapor and make it clear.
translations.idiomatic: [254] I order Lord Basket to bottle the gale and boil the haze until the air runs clean.

sentences.zh: [255]曳雲旗之離離兮，鳴玉鸞之譻譻。

translations.literal: [255] Cloud banners trail, long and streaming; jade simurghs on the yoke cry clear and sweet.
translations.idiomatic: [255] Cloud standards trail like silk; jade phoenixes on the shafts cry silver notes.

sentences.zh: [256]涉清霄而升遐兮，浮蔑蒙而上征。

translations.literal: [256] I ford clear empyrean and rise afar; I float through thin mist and climb on high.
translations.idiomatic: [256] I cross the lucid sky and climb beyond; I drift through gauze-mist toward the height.

sentences.zh: [257]紛翼翼以徐戾兮，焱回回其揚靈。

translations.literal: [257] Thick wings beat slowly as we settle; blazing spirits wheel and flaunt their power.
translations.idiomatic: [257] Wing on wing we glide to rest; spirits of fire whirl and show their force.

sentences.zh: [258]叫帝閽使辟扉兮，覿天皇於瓊宮。

translations.literal: [258] I shout to the heaven gatekeeper to open the doors; I behold the Celestial King in his jade palace.
translations.idiomatic: [258] I thunder at the celestial warder to swing the gates and face the High King in his jade hall.

sentences.zh: [259]聆廣樂之九奏兮，展洩洩以肜肜。

translations.literal: [259] I listen to the ninefold strains of the great music; relaxed and easy, the harmony spreads wide.
translations.idiomatic: [259] I hear the nine movements of the cosmic symphony; the sound rolls out, easy and vast.

sentences.zh: [260]考理亂於律鈞兮，意建始而思終。

translations.literal: [260] I examine order and chaos on the scale of pitch; my thought runs from the founding to the end.
translations.idiomatic: [260] I weigh peace and turmoil on the tuning-fork of law, thinking from the world's first note to its close.

sentences.zh: [261]惟盤逸之無斁兮，懼樂往而哀來。

translations.literal: [261] Only dissolute ease knows no satiety; I fear that when joy passes, grief will come.
translations.idiomatic: [261] Endless revel never knows its fill; I dread the sorrow that follows when music stops.

sentences.zh: [262]素撫弦而餘音兮，大容吟曰念哉。

translations.literal: [262] Su gently plucks the strings, leaving lingering tone; Grand Harmony sings, Remember! Remember!
translations.idiomatic: [262] Plain Su strums the zither into silence; the great chorus chants, Remember, remember!

sentences.zh: [263]既防溢而靜志兮，迨我暇以□翔。

translations.literal: [263] Having guarded against excess and calmed my will, I seize my leisure to wander free.
translations.idiomatic: [263] Having stemmed overflow and stilled my mind, I use the pause to roam at will.

sentences.zh: [264]出紫宮之肅肅兮，集大微之閬閬。

translations.literal: [264] I leave the Purple Palace's solemn hush; I gather in the Grand Tenuity's vast court.
translations.idiomatic: [264] I quit the Purple Palace's hush and alight in the wide forum of Grand Tenuity.

sentences.zh: [265]命王良掌策駟兮，踰高閣之鏘鏘。

translations.literal: [265] I command Wang Liang to hold the four-in-hand; we pass the high tower with a clangor of jade.
translations.idiomatic: [265] I set Wang Liang at the reins; we clatter past the high gate in a jade chorus.

sentences.zh: [266]建罔車之幕幕兮，獵青林之芒芒。

translations.literal: [266] I stretch the net-chariot's curtain wide; I hunt the green forest's endless expanse.
translations.idiomatic: [266] I spread the star-net's awning and course the green forest's boundless shade.

sentences.zh: [267]彎威弧之撥剌兮，射嶓頤之封狼。

translations.literal: [267] I bend the mighty bow with a crack; I shoot the giant wolf at Bo's jaw.
translations.idiomatic: [267] I bend the sky bow with a snap and loose at the great wolf on Bo's height.

sentences.zh: [268]觀壁壘於北落兮，伐河鼓之磅硠。

translations.literal: [268] I view the ramparts at Beiluo; I strike the River Drum with a booming roll.
translations.idiomatic: [268] I scan the bastions at Beiluo and pound the River Drum until it roars.

sentences.zh: [269]乘天潢之泛泛兮，浮雲漢之湯湯。

translations.literal: [269] I ride the Celestial Ford's broad flood; I float the Milky Way's surging stream.
translations.idiomatic: [269] I ford the Silver River's shoals and drift the Milky Way's rushing tide.

sentences.zh: [270]倚招搖、攝提以低回□流兮，察二紀、五緯之綢繆遹皇。

translations.literal: [270] I lean on Dubhe and Sheti, wheeling and drifting; I watch the two sequences and five weavings, tangled and ever bright.
translations.idiomatic: [270] I rest on Dubhe and Sheti, circling in slow drift, and study the paired cycles and five cords of fate, netted in light.

sentences.zh: [271]偃蹇夭矯彧以連捲兮，雜沓叢□颯以方驤。

translations.literal: [271] Towering, twisting, lushly they twine; crowded, clustered, they rush in squadrons.
translations.idiomatic: [271] Spirits rear and coil in luxuriant knots; hosts crowd past in gusty squadrons.

sentences.zh: [272]□汨飂戾沛以罔象兮，爛漫麗靡□以迭逿。

translations.literal: [272] Rushing winds and sharp gusts pour like formless waves; splendor and sheen overlap in endless play.
translations.idiomatic: [272] Wild winds and biting gales flood like shapeless surf; radiance and shimmer chase one another without end.

sentences.zh: [273]凌驚雷之□糝兮，弄狂電之淫裔。

translations.literal: [273] I leap the shattering thunder's crackling rain; I toy with mad lightning's trailing skirts.
translations.idiomatic: [273] I vault the thunder's splintering hail and toy with lightning's whipping skirts.

sentences.zh: [274]踰庬澒於宕冥兮，貫倒景而高厲。

translations.literal: [274] I cross the vast gulf in boundless dark; I pierce inverted light and soar on high.
translations.idiomatic: [274] I span the formless void and pierce the upside-down radiance, climbing beyond.

sentences.zh: [275]廓蕩蕩其無涯兮，乃今窮乎天外。

translations.literal: [275] Vast, empty, without a shore—only now have I reached beyond heaven's edge.
translations.idiomatic: [275] Empty, endless, without rim—at last I stand outside the sky's last wall.

sentences.zh: 據開陽而俯盼兮，臨舊鄉之暗藹。

translations.literal: I rest on Kaiyang and look down; I gaze on my old home, dim and hazy.
translations.idiomatic: I lean from the star Kaiyang and peer downward; my homeland lies faint in the blue haze.

sentences.zh: [276]悲離居之勞心兮，情悁悁而思歸。

translations.literal: [276] I grieve that parted dwelling wearies the heart; my mood is anxious and longs to return.
translations.idiomatic: [276] Exile gnaws the heart; my breast tightens with homesickness.

sentences.zh: [277]魂眷眷而屢顧兮，馬倚輈而俳回。

translations.literal: [277] My soul turns again and again to look back; the horse halts at the shaft, pacing in circles.
translations.idiomatic: [277] My spirit keeps glancing homeward; the horses circle the traces, unwilling to go on.

sentences.zh: [278]雖遨遊以偷樂兮，豈愁慕之可懷。

translations.literal: [278] Though I roam in stolen pleasure, how can anxious longing be cherished?
translations.idiomatic: [278] However far I wander for a moment's ease, how can I hug this ache of longing?

sentences.zh: [279]出閶闔兮降天塗，乘蹣忽兮馳虛無。

translations.literal: [279] I leave Changhe and descend the sky-road; riding blurried speed I gallop through the void.
translations.idiomatic: [279] I slip from Changhe's gate down the sky-ladder and race blur-eyed through emptiness.

sentences.zh: [280]雲霏霏兮繞余輪，風眇眇兮震余旟。

translations.literal: [280] Clouds weave thick about my wheels; the wind's thin cry shakes my banners.
translations.idiomatic: [280] Cloud skeins wrap my wheels; thin wind screams in the streaming flags.

sentences.zh: 繽聯翩兮紛暗曖，倏眩眃兮反常閭。

translations.literal: Colors link and flutter in dim confusion; in a flash, dizzy, I return to my old lane.
translations.idiomatic: Hues braid and blur in smoky whirl; one dizzy blink—and I stand again in my own lane.

translations.literal: [281] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [281] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 收疇昔之逸豫兮，卷淫放之遐心。

translations.literal: I gather up former ease; I roll away the wandering heart of licentious release.
translations.idiomatic: I fold away past indulgence and furl the heart that roved in reckless drift.

sentences.zh: [282]修初服之娑娑兮，長余佩之參參。

translations.literal: [282] I trim my first garments, trailing long; I lengthen my pendants, hanging deep.
translations.idiomatic: [282] I straighten the robes I first wore, sweeping long hems, and let my girdle-gems fall deep.

sentences.zh: [283]文章煥以粲爛兮，美紛紜以從風。

translations.literal: [283] Pattern and color blaze in splendor; beauty in confusion follows the wind.
translations.idiomatic: [283] Brocade and hue flare brilliant; a tangle of grace streams on the breeze.

sentences.zh: 御六蓺之珍駕兮，游道德之平林。

translations.literal: I drive the six arts' precious carriage; I roam the level forest of the Way and its power.
translations.idiomatic: I yoke the chariot of the six classical arts and wander the even grove of the Way.

sentences.zh: [284]結典籍而為□兮，歐儒、墨而為禽。

translations.literal: [284] I knot the classics as my snare; I hunt Confucians and Mohists as quarry.
translations.idiomatic: [284] I knot the canon into nets and chase down Ruist and Mohist doctrine like game.

sentences.zh: [285]玩陰陽之變化兮，詠雅、頌之徽音。

translations.literal: [285] I toy with yin and yang's transformations; I chant the elegant sounds of Odes and Hymns.
translations.idiomatic: [285] I trace yin and yang in play and intone the subtle music of Airs and Eulogies.

sentences.zh: 嘉曾氏之歸耕兮，慕歷陵之欽崟。

translations.literal: I praise the Zeng clan's return to plowing; I admire Lilin's steep heights.
translations.idiomatic: I honor Zengzi's choice to go home to the plow and envy the sheer cliffs above Lilin.

sentences.zh: [286]共夙昔而不貳兮，居終始之所服也；

translations.literal: [286] Together from days of old without wavering; this is what I wear from first to last.
translations.idiomatic: [286] What I swore at dawn I keep at dusk; this is the robe I mean to wear from birth to death.

sentences.zh: 夕惕若厲以省□兮，懼余身之未□也。

translations.literal: Evening-tense as facing peril, I examine my faults; I fear my person is not yet complete.
translations.idiomatic: Each night I wake like a man on a cliff, auditing my faults, afraid I am not yet whole.

sentences.zh: [287]苟中情之端直兮，莫吾知而不恧。

translations.literal: [287] If the inner heart is straight, though none know me I feel no shame.
translations.idiomatic: [287] If the heart's core stays true, I need no witness to be free of shame.

sentences.zh: [288]墨無為以凝志兮，與仁義乎消搖。

translations.literal: [288] I ink non-action to fix my will; with humaneness and righteousness I wander at ease.
translations.idiomatic: [288] I fix my mind in quiet non-striving and let humaneness and right carry me at ease.

sentences.zh: [289]不出戶而知天下兮，何必歷遠以劬勞？

translations.literal: [289] Without leaving the door I know the realm—why must I travel far in bitter toil?
translations.idiomatic: [289] The world may be known without crossing the sill—why rack the body on endless roads?

translations.literal: [290] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [290] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 系曰：天長地久歲不留，俟河之清祗懷憂。

translations.literal: Envoi: Heaven is long, earth enduring, yet years will not wait; waiting for the river to clear only heaps sorrow.
translations.idiomatic: The coda says: Heaven endures, earth lasts, yet the years will not stay; to wait for the Yellow River to run clear is only to pile grief.

sentences.zh: [291]願得遠度以自娛，上下無常窮六區。

translations.literal: [291] I would cross far distances to please myself; above and below there is no constancy through the six regions.
translations.idiomatic: [291] I would roam beyond measure for my soul's sake, for up and down, in all six directions, nothing holds still.

sentences.zh: [292]超踰騰躍絕世俗，躑□神舉逞所欲。

translations.literal: [292] Leaping beyond, vaulting past, I cut off the vulgar world; pacing, rising in spirit, I indulge my desire.
translations.idiomatic: [292] I vault beyond the common dust; my spirit rears and takes whatever path it wills.

sentences.zh: 天不可階仙夫希，□舟悄悄吝不飛。

translations.literal: Heaven has no stair; the immortal is rare; my lone boat drifts silent, grudging to fly.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven has no ladder; the transcendent is seldom met; my solitary skiff drifts, reluctant to rise.

translations.literal: [293] Song and Prince Qiao stand on high—who can part from them?
translations.idiomatic: [293] Song and Wangzi Qiao tower above mortality—who would not cling to such companions?

translations.literal: Knotting my essence, I wander far and let the heart be carried away.
translations.idiomatic: I bind my vital essence to distant roaming and surrender the heart to the journey.

sentences.zh: [294]回志朅來從玄諆，[295]獲我所求夫何思！

translations.literal: [294] I turn my will and come back, following the dark counsels; [295] having won what I sought, what more is there to brood?
translations.idiomatic: [294] I wheel my purpose homeward, heeding the shadowed oracles; [295] once I hold what I sought, why should the mind still ache?

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yonghe, he went out as chancellor of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: In the first year of Yonghe (136 CE) he was posted out as Chancellor of Hejian kingdom.

translations.literal: [296] At that time the king of the state was arrogant and extravagant and did not obey statutes and precedents;
translations.idiomatic: [296] The prince of Hejian lived in swaggering excess and trampled the law;

translations.literal: there were moreover many powerful magnates who together acted contrary to rule.
translations.idiomatic: powerful local clans joined him in lawless schemes.

sentences.zh: 衡下車，治威嚴，整法度，陰知奸黨名姓，一時收禽，上下肅然，稱為政理。

translations.literal: When Heng stepped from his carriage he established awesome dignity, set law and order in order, secretly learned the names of treacherous factions, and in one stroke seized them all; high and low stood in awe and called it good government.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng arrived, imposed stern discipline, restored the statutes, mapped every conspirator by name, then swept them up in a single stroke; the whole kingdom fell silent and praised his rule.

sentences.zh: 視事三年，上書乞骸骨，徵拜尚書。

translations.literal: After three years in office he submitted a memorial begging leave for his bones; he was summoned and appointed Minister of the Masters of Writing.
translations.idiomatic: After three years he asked to retire; the court recalled him instead as Minister of the Masters of Writing.

translations.literal: At age sixty-two he died in the fourth year of Yonghe.
translations.idiomatic: He died at sixty-two in Yonghe 4 (139 CE).

sentences.zh: 著《周官訓詁》，崔瑗以為不能有異於諸儒也。

translations.literal: He composed Exegetical Glosses on the Offices of Zhou; Cui Yuan held that it could not differ from the various Confucians.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote glosses on the Rites of Zhou; Cui Yuan judged the work indistinguishable from other Ru scholars' commentaries.

sentences.zh: 又欲繼孔子易說彖、象殘缺者，竟不能就。

translations.literal: He also wished to continue Confucius's explanations of the Changes where the Tuan and Xiang commentaries were fragmentary, but in the end could not finish.
translations.idiomatic: He hoped to complete Confucius's lost glosses on the Book of Changes for the Tuan and Xiang sections, but never brought the task to an end.

sentences.zh: 所著詩、賦、銘、七言、靈憲、應閒、七辯、巡誥、懸圖凡三十二篇。

translations.literal: What he wrote—poems, rhapsodies, inscriptions, heptasyllabic verse, Lingxian, Response to Idle Criticism, Seven Arguments, Patrol Admonitions, and Hanging Charts—comes to thirty-two pieces in all.
translations.idiomatic: His surviving writings—poetry, fu, inscriptions, heptasyllabic lines, the Lingxian, the Response to Idle Criticism, the Seven Debates, the Patrol Admonition, and the Hanging Chart—total thirty-two titles.

translations.literal: [297] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [297] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 永初中，謁者僕射劉珍、校書郎劉騊駼等著作東觀，撰集漢記，因定漢家禮儀，上言請衡參論其事，會並卒，而衡常歎息，欲終成之。

translations.literal: In the Yongchu era, the Herald Director Liu Zhen, collator Liu Taotu, and others compiled the Han record at the Eastern Lodge and, while fixing Han ritual, submitted a memorial asking Heng to join their deliberations; they all died together, and Heng often sighed, wishing to finish the work.
translations.idiomatic: During Yongchu, Herald Director Liu Zhen and Collator Liu Taotu were drafting the Han annals at the Eastern Institute and, while codifying court ritual, asked Zhang Heng to join the project; the colleagues died before it was done, and Zhang Heng often sighed that he still hoped to complete their work.

sentences.zh: 及為侍中，上疏請得專事東觀，收撿遺文，畢力補綴。

translations.literal: When he became Palace Attendant, he submitted a memorial asking to devote himself to the Eastern Lodge, gather scattered documents, and exhaust his strength patching them.
translations.idiomatic: Once Palace Attendant, he begged leave to work solely at the Eastern Institute, recover stray documents, and stitch the archive back together.

sentences.zh: [298]又條上司馬遷、班固所□與典籍不合者十餘事。

translations.literal: [298] He also itemized more than ten matters in which Sima Qian and Ban Gu disagreed with the canonical texts.
translations.idiomatic: [298] He listed a dozen places where Sima Qian and Ban Gu parted company with the received classics.

sentences.zh: [299]又以為王莽本傳但應載篡事而已，至於編年月，紀災祥，宜為元后本紀。

translations.literal: [299] He further held that Wang Mang's basic annals should record only the usurpation; as for arranging years and months and noting omens, that belonged in a basic annals for the Empress Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: [299] He argued that Wang Mang's annals should confine themselves to the seizure of power, while chronology and portents belonged in a separate annals for Empress Dowager Yuan.

sentences.zh: 又更始居位，人無異望，光武初為其將，然後即真，宜以更始之號建於光武之初。

translations.literal: Moreover, when Gengshi held the throne the people had no other expectation; Guangwu at first was his general and only afterward assumed the true position—it would be fitting to place Gengshi's reign-title at the beginning of Guangwu's rise.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Xuan ruled, the realm expected no other savior; Liu Xiu first served as his general before taking the true mandate—Zhang Heng urged historians to set Gengshi's era at the opening of Guangwu's story.

translations.literal: He submitted these writings several times, but in the end they were not heeded.
translations.idiomatic: He sent these memorials again and again, yet the court never acted.

sentences.zh: 及後之著述，多不詳典，時人追恨之。

translations.literal: Later writings mostly did not follow canonical models, and people of the time regretted this in retrospect.
translations.idiomatic: Later histories ignored his models, and later scholars looked back with regret.

sentences.zh: 論曰：崔瑗之稱平子曰「數術窮天地，製作侔造化」。

translations.literal: The essayist remarks: Cui Yuan's praise of Pingzi said, "His numerology exhausts heaven and earth; his craft rivals creation itself."
translations.idiomatic: The historian comments: Cui Yuan said of Zhang Heng that his calculations spanned heaven and earth and his inventions rivaled nature's own.

translations.literal: [300] Can such achievement be put into words?
translations.idiomatic: [300] Can words even compass such achievement?

sentences.zh: 推其圍范兩儀，天地無所蘊其靈；

translations.literal: Pushing his scope to embrace the two modes, heaven and earth could not hide their numinous power;
translations.idiomatic: He drew a circle wide enough to hold yin and yang, so that heaven and earth could not veil their spirits from him;

translations.literal: [301] exercising feeling and mechanism on things, the living could not match his intelligence.
translations.idiomatic: [301] yet when he turned mind and machine on the world, no living scholar could match his wit.

translations.literal: Therefore—
translations.idiomatic: Therefore—

translations.literal: (gloss: zhi, wisdom)
translations.idiomatic: (The apparatus supplies the character read as wisdom.)

translations.literal: *Knowledge* draws on the deepest subtlety: that is the highest art of man.
translations.idiomatic: —to let knowledge sound the deepest recesses is the highest mastery a man can wield.

sentences.zh: 記曰：「德成而上，蓺成而下。

translations.literal: The Record says: "When virtue is complete it rises above; when craft is complete it falls below."
translations.idiomatic: The Record says: moral completion lifts a man upward; mere technical mastery keeps him low.

translations.literal: " [303] Measuring this thought, is it mere craft?
translations.idiomatic: [303] Weigh that judgment—is this only craftsmanship?

translations.literal: How could it injure virtue!
translations.idiomatic: Surely it cannot diminish true virtue.

translations.literal: [304] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [304] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：三才理通，人靈多蔽。

translations.literal: Encomium: The three powers share one pattern, yet human spirits are often blinded.
translations.idiomatic: The eulogy reads: Heaven, earth, and man share one pattern, yet the human mind clouds so easily.

translations.literal: [305] Near at hand he pushed shape and reckoning; far away he drew up what lay sunk in obscurity.
translations.idiomatic: [305] Close by he pressed geometry and number; far off he hauled truth from the murk.

translations.literal: Without dark pondering, who could make it shine?
translations.idiomatic: Without such brooding depth, who could have lit the dark?

translations.literal: [306] Editorial commentary marker in the source; no additional narrative text.
translations.idiomatic: [306] The line marks an editorial note in the received text.

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Collation notes

sentences.zh: 一八九八頁五行 以三十*

translations.literal: Page 1898, line 5: "using thirty-*
translations.idiomatic: Critical note, p. 1898, line 5: the text reads thirty with a lacuna.

translations.literal: (five)
translations.idiomatic: (The received graph is five.)

sentences.zh: *［六］蓍揲之 按：刊誤謂太玄乃用三十六揲，作「五」誤。

translations.literal: *[six] stalks for milfoil divination." The Kanwu observes that the Taixuan uses thirty-six stalks, so writing "five" is an error.
translations.idiomatic: The emendation to six yarrow stalks follows Kanwu, since the Taixuan uses thirty-six, making five a scribal error.

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The text is emended accordingly.

sentences.zh: 一八九九頁一一行 昔有文王 按：刊誤謂「昔有」當作「昔者」。

translations.literal: Page 1899, line 11: "Long ago there was King Wen." The Kanwu says "there was" should read "in olden days."
translations.idiomatic: P. 1899, line 11: Kanwu argues xiyou should read xizhe for King Wen line.

translations.literal: Page 1900, line 9: "bent, then—*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1900, line 9: the Laozi quotation begins with a lacuna after bent.

translations.literal: (upright)
translations.idiomatic: (Gloss: upright.)

translations.literal: *[straight] According to the Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Emended to straight per Ji and Palace editions.

translations.literal: Note: the received Laozi reads "straight."
translations.idiomatic: Modern Laozi editions read straight.

sentences.zh: 一九〇一頁三行 金聲而玉振［之］ 據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 2: "metal sound and jade vibration [of it]" supplied from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 2: the object particle is restored from Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一九〇二頁二行 不到 汲本、殿本「到」作「至」。

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 2: "not arriving"—Ji and Palace editions read "not reaching" for dao.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 2: dao emended to zhi following Ji and Palace texts.

translations.literal: Note: the received Mencius reads "reach."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius editions read reach.

sentences.zh: 一九〇二頁三行 趙岐注云 按：「岐」原作「歧」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 3: "Zhao Qi notes"—the character Qi was written qi with the mountain radical; corrected outright.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 3: Zhao Qi's name corrected from wrong qi graph.

translations.literal: Same below.
translations.idiomatic: The same correction applies below.

sentences.zh: 一九〇二頁四行 君子固窮 按：「固」原作「困」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 4: "the nobleman is secure in want"—"secure" was written "trapped"; corrected outright.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 4: gu trapped emended to gu secure per Analects usage.

sentences.zh: 一九〇二頁四行 如何君子不*

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 4: "how could the nobleman not—*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 4: lacuna before await in Mencius quotation.

translations.literal: (wait)
translations.idiomatic: (Gloss: wait.)

sentences.zh: *［待］其招而妄見也 據汲本改，與今本孟子趙注合。

translations.literal: *[await] his summons yet rashly appear" emended per Ji edition to match the modern Mencius with Zhao commentary.
translations.idiomatic: Await emended from Ji edition to match Mencius Zhao commentary.

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 8: "*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 8: lacuna in quotation.

translations.literal: (ye)
translations.idiomatic: (Particle ye.)

sentences.zh: *［曰］我狐父之人丘也 據刊誤改。

translations.literal: *[said] I am the man of Hufu, Qiu" emended per Kanwu.
translations.idiomatic: Supplied said per Kanwu for the Zuo quotation.

sentences.zh: 一九〇二頁一三行 是以貨賄*

translations.literal: Page 1902, line 13: "therefore bribes*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1902, line 13: bribes line has lacuna.

translations.literal: (particle suo)
translations.idiomatic: (Erroneous suo particle.)

translations.literal: *take me"—deleted per Kanwu.
translations.idiomatic: Superfluous suo deleted per Kanwu.

sentences.zh: 按：今本孟子趙注無「所」字。

translations.literal: Note: the modern Mencius with Zhao commentary lacks the particle suo.
translations.idiomatic: Modern Mencius Zhao commentary lacks suo.

sentences.zh: 一九〇三頁六行 有人［之］不理 據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1903, line 6: "some people [it] were not governed" supplied from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1903, line 6: restored zhi particle per Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一九〇四頁一五行 奚冀其二哉 按：「冀」原作「異」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1904, line 15: "how dare we hope for its second"—"hope" was written "different"; corrected per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1904, line 15: ji hope corrected from yi different per Ji and Palace.

sentences.zh: 一九〇五頁九行 黿鳴岐野 按：「岐」原作「歧」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1905, line 9: "turtle crying on Qi wilds"—"Qi" was written with the wrong qi; corrected outright.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1905, line 9: Qi place name corrected from wrong qi graph.

sentences.zh: 一九〇五頁一三行 黃帝乃下天女曰*

translations.literal: Page 1905, line 13: "The Yellow Emperor then sent down a heaven girl named*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1905, line 13: lacuna in name of heaven-sent girl.

translations.literal: (demon)
translations.idiomatic: (Graph read as demon.)

sentences.zh: **[□]* 集解引沉欽韓說，謂「妖」乃「□」之鬥。

translations.literal: **[ba]* The Jijie cites Shen Qinhan: the character demon is a corruption of ba.
translations.idiomatic: Jijie cites Shen Qinhan: demon is a miswriting of ba drought demon.

sentences.zh: 按：下雲妖亦魃也，音步末反，則為「□」字之鬥無疑，今據改。

translations.literal: Note: below it says the demon is also the drought demon, pronounced bomo fan, so the corruption to ba is certain; now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary identifies the graph as ba; emended accordingly.

translations.literal: Same below.
translations.idiomatic: Same emendation below.

sentences.zh: 一九〇七頁四行 孟子曰阿意事貴脅肩所尊俗之情也 按：沉家本謂此疑孟子注家語，或孟子逸文也。

translations.literal: Page 1907, line 4: "Mencius says: to curry favor with the great, hunched shoulders before the honored, is the vulgar feeling." Shen Jiaben thinks this may be a glossator's words or lost Mencius text.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1907, line 4: Shen Jiaben treats the line as possible Mencius gloss or lost fragment.

sentences.zh: 一九〇八頁一一行 以牒為械 按：御覽三三六引「牒」作「褋」，書鈔引斗作「襟」。

translations.literal: Page 1908, line 11: "using strips as fetters"—the Imperial Readings III 36 cite die as die (garment); the Shuchao cites a variant reading as lapel.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1908, line 11: Imperial Readings and Shuchao variant graphs for wooden strips.

sentences.zh: 孫詒讓墨子閒詁謂作「褋」是。

translations.literal: Sun Yirang's Mozi interlinear notes say die is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Sun Yirang's Mozi notes favor die.

sentences.zh: 俞樾謂牒、褋皆□字，其本字當作「梜」，梜即箸也，孫氏謂俞說亦通。

translations.literal: Yu Yue says die and die are both wrong characters; the original should be jia, meaning chopsticks; Sun says Yu's view is also plausible.
translations.idiomatic: Yu Yue argues the original word is jia chopsticks; Sun allows both views.

sentences.zh: 一九〇八頁一三行 杖節臥起 按：汲本「杖」作「持」。

translations.literal: Page 1908, line 13: "holding the baton lying and rising"—Ji and Palace editions read chi hold for zhang staff.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1908, line 13: chi hold vs zhang staff variant between editions.

sentences.zh: 校補謂注專就臥起言，故云持節，若改作「杖」，則臥豈能杖，作「杖」非。

translations.literal: The Jiaobu says the commentary refers only to lying and rising, hence chi; if changed to staff, one cannot staff while lying—staff is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Jiaobu defends chi because one cannot hold a staff while reclining.

sentences.zh: 一九〇九頁三行 孔安國以為三墳*

translations.literal: Page 1909, line 3: "Kong Anguo thought the Three Mounds*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1909, line 3: Kong Anguo on Three Mounds lacuna.

translations.literal: (Five Canons)
translations.idiomatic: (Gloss: Five Canons.)

translations.literal: *books of the Three August Ones" deleted per Jiaobu.
translations.idiomatic: Redundant phrase deleted per Jiaobu.

sentences.zh: 一九一〇頁三行 宜獲福祉神祇 按：集解引蘇輿說，謂「福」「祉」疑衍一字。

translations.literal: Page 1910, line 3: "It is fitting to obtain blessing from the spirits"—the Jijie citing Su Yu suspects one of fu or zhi is redundant.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1910, line 3: Su Yu suspects duplicate in blessing phrase.

sentences.zh: 一九一〇頁四行 冥鑒在茲 汲本、殿本「冥」作「宜」。

translations.literal: Page 1910, line 4: "silent mirror is here"—Ji and Palace editions read yi should for ming dark.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1910, line 4: ming vs yi variant in editions.

sentences.zh: 按：嚴可均輯全後漢文作「冥」。

translations.literal: Note: Yan Kejun's collected Later Han prose reads ming.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Kejun's anthology keeps ming.

sentences.zh: 一九一〇頁一〇行 僭恆陽若 按：汲本「陽」作「暘」。

translations.literal: Page 1910, line 10: "presumption brings constant yang like this"—Ji edition reads yang as bright yang for the solar omen.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1910, line 10: yang vs yang bright variant.

translations.literal: Commentary same.
translations.idiomatic: Commentary follows same variant.

sentences.zh: 一九一〇頁一五行 □消不至矣 按：□消不至，語意重復，疑當依袁宏紀作「□沴不至」。

translations.literal: Page 1910, line 15: "omens dissolve and do not arrive"—the wording repeats; suspect it should follow Yuan Hong's annals as "baleful qi will not arrive."
translations.idiomatic: P. 1910, line 15: suspected duplication; Yuan Hong reads baleful qi.

sentences.zh: 又按：袁宏紀引張衡此疏多異文，今不列舉。

translations.literal: Further note: Yuan Hong's annals quote Zhang Heng's memorial with many variant graphs, not listed here.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong variants for this memorial are omitted here.

sentences.zh: 一九一一頁二行 揚雄 按：前後皆作「楊雄」，「楊」字從木，獨此篇注文皆從□作「揚」，今依原本，不改歸一律。

translations.literal: Page 1911, line 2: "Yang Xiong"—elsewhere the text writes Yang with the wood radical; only this chapter's notes use the hand radical yang; the original is kept without forcing uniformity.
translations.idiomatic: P. 1911, line 2: Yang Xiong spelling left as in source despite mixed forms.

sentences.zh: 一九一二頁一五行 中央者*

translations.literal: Page 1912, line 15: "the central region is*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1912, line 15: lacuna in cosmograph line.

translations.literal: (earth spirit)
translations.idiomatic: (Gloss: earth spirit.)

sentences.zh: **[北辰]*之所居 汲本、殿本「地」作「北」，王先謙謂當作「北辰」，今據改。

translations.literal: **[where the North Star]* dwells"—Ji and Palace editions read di earth as bei north; Wang Xianqian says it should be the North Pole; now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The reading for the North Star was adopted on Wang Xianqian's argument and the Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一九一三頁二行 所以*

translations.literal: Page 1913, line 2: "therefore*"
translations.idiomatic: P. 1913, line 2: therefore with following lacuna.

translations.literal: (Gloss: ) (See Chinese subcommentary for full quotation.)
translations.idiomatic: The critical apparatus marks ().

translations.literal: Collation: *［］ 、。
translations.idiomatic: Emended per the Ji and Palace editions: *［］ 、。

sentences.zh: 一九一三頁七行 有詔使劉向及子歆於秘書 汲本「於」作「為」。

translations.literal: Collation: 「」「」。
translations.idiomatic: At p. 1913, line 7, collation entry: 「」「」。

translations.literal: Note: 「」. (See Chinese subcommentary for full quotation.)
translations.idiomatic: Editorial comment: 「」.

sentences.zh: 校補引柳從辰說，謂當依前書向傳作「領校秘書」，「為」字即「領」字轉寫之鬥，「於」字又明明「校」字形近之鬥，兩本固皆有脫斗也。

translations.literal: The Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen, arguing that the line should follow the Former Han biography of Liu Xiang as "supervising collation in the secretariat"; the graph wei is a miscopy of ling, yu is a graphic confusion with jiao, and both woodblock editions drop characters.
translations.idiomatic: The editorial supplement follows Liu Congchen: restore "supervise the secretariat" as in Liu Xiang's biography; wei and yu are miswritten for ling and jiao, and both printed texts are defective.

sentences.zh: 「中黃伯曰：『余左執太行之獶，而右搏雕虎，唯象之未與，吾心試焉。

translations.literal: "Zhong Huangbo said: 'In my left hand I hold the Taihang boar, in my right I strike the carved tiger; only the elephant I have not matched—my heart would try it.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhong Huangbo boasts that he has mastered boar and tiger but still longs to test himself against an elephant.

sentences.zh: 有力者則又願為牛，欲與象鬥，以自試。

translations.literal: A strong man then wishes to be an ox, desiring to fight the elephant in order to test himself.
translations.idiomatic: The strong man even volunteers as an ox, eager to grapple the elephant and prove his strength.

sentences.zh: 今二三子以為義矣，將惡乎試之？

translations.literal: Now you gentlemen already deem yourselves righteous—how then will you put it to the test?
translations.idiomatic: Since you call yourselves righteous, where will you find a trial stern enough?

translations.literal: For poverty and want are the Taihang boar;
translations.idiomatic: Want and poverty are your Taihang boar—

sentences.zh: 疏賤，義之雕虎也。而吾日遇之，亦足以試矣。』」

translations.literal: "Low station and humble birth are the carved tiger of duty; yet I meet them daily—that is trial enough.'"
translations.idiomatic: obscurity and low rank are the carved tiger of right; I meet them every day, and that is ordeal enough.'"

translations.literal: Note: the Jiaobu cites Liu Congchen, saying both yuan and chu should read ju; the commentary quotes Zheng Xuan's gloss on the Record of Rites; though it does not name the chapter, the Qu li note on "do not stamp the foot" reads "moving in haste."
translations.idiomatic: Editors argue yuan and chu are miswritings for ju and align the gloss with Zheng Xuan on the Qu li passage about not stamping the foot in haste.
