sentences.zh: 西鄂，縣，故城在今鄧州向城縣南，有平子墓及碑在焉，崔瑗之文也。

translations.literal: Xi'e was a county; its old walled town lay south of Xiangcheng in Dengzhou, where Zhang Heng's tomb and stele still stand, inscribed with a text by Cui Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Xi'e county's old site lies south of present Xiangcheng in Dengzhou; Zhang Heng's grave and inscribed stele survive there, with an epitaph by Cui Yuan.

sentences.zh: 桓譚《新論》曰：「揚雄作玄書，以為玄者，天也，道也。

translations.literal: Huan Tan's New Discourses says: "Yang Xiong wrote the Mystery, holding that Mystery means Heaven and means the Way."
translations.idiomatic: Huan Tan's New Discourses records Yang Xiong's view that the Xuan scripture names both Heaven and the Way.

sentences.zh: 言聖賢製法作事，皆引天道以為本統，而因附續萬類﹑王政﹑人事﹑法度，故宓羲氏謂之易，老子謂之道，孔子謂之元，而揚雄謂之玄。

translations.literal: It means that when sages and worthies establish models and undertake affairs, they all take Heaven's Way as their root and thread, then attach and continue the myriad kinds, royal government, human affairs, and legal norms; therefore Fuxi called it the Changes, Laozi called it the Way, Confucius called it the Originating Power, and Yang Xiong called it the Mystery.
translations.idiomatic: Sages ground law and action in Heaven's pattern, then extend it through nature, statecraft, and statute—hence Fuxi's Changes, Laozi's Way, Confucius's Originating Power, and Yang Xiong's Mystery are one lineage under different names.

sentences.zh: 玄經三篇，以紀天地人之道，立三體有上中下，如禹貢之陳三品。

translations.literal: The Mystery scripture has three sections, recording the Way of heaven, earth, and man; it sets up three forms with upper, middle, and lower, like the Yu gong's arrangement of three grades.
translations.idiomatic: The Xuan is in three parts for heaven, earth, and man, each with upper, middle, and lower tiers—analogous to the three grades in the Tribute of Yu.

sentences.zh: 三三而九，因以九九八十一，故為八十一卦。

translations.literal: Three threes make nine; therefore by nine nines eighty-one, so it becomes eighty-one "hexagrams."
translations.idiomatic: Three times three yields nine, and nine times nine yields eighty-one, which is why the work frames eighty-one symbolic figures.

sentences.zh: 以四為數，數從一至四，重累變易，竟八十一而□，不可損益。

translations.literal: It takes four as the number; numbers run from one to four, piling and changing, finishing at eighty-one and stopping, without increase or decrease.
translations.idiomatic: The numerology works in fours, cycling from one through four until the pattern closes at eighty-one, neither adding nor subtracting a step.

translations.literal: Using thirty-*
translations.idiomatic: The commentary continues "using thirty-" with a lacuna before the next graph.

translations.literal: (Gloss: ) (See Chinese subcommentary for full quotation.)
translations.idiomatic: The critical apparatus marks ().

translations.literal: Collation: *［］。
translations.idiomatic: Collation entry: *［］。

sentences.zh: 玄經五千餘言，而傳十二篇也。」

translations.literal: The Mystery scripture runs to more than five thousand words, while its commentary fills twelve chapters."
translations.idiomatic: The Xuan text exceeds five thousand graphs, with twelve chapters of traditional commentary closing the quotation.

sentences.zh: 子雲當哀帝時著太玄經，自漢初至哀帝，二百歲也。

translations.literal: Ziyun composed the Mystery in Emperor Ai's reign; from the Han founding down to Emperor Ai is two hundred years.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Xiong finished the Taixuan under Emperor Ai; two centuries separate Han's rise from that reign.

sentences.zh: 自中興至獻帝，一百八十九年也。

translations.literal: From the restoration down to Emperor Xian is one hundred eighty-nine years.
translations.idiomatic: From Guangwu's restoration to the last Han emperor spans one hundred eighty-nine years.

sentences.zh: 自此已上，並衡與崔瑗書之文也。

translations.literal: From this upward, all is text from Heng's letter to Cui Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Everything above this point comes from Zhang Heng's letter to Cui Yuan.

sentences.zh: 漢官儀「太史令屬太常，秩六百石」也。

translations.literal: The Han Official Ceremonies says: "The Prefect Grand Astrologer belongs to the Minister of Ceremonies, rank six hundred piculs."
translations.idiomatic: Han guanyi lists the Prefect Grand Astrologer under the Minister of Ceremonies at six hundred piculs salary.

sentences.zh: 漢名臣奏曰，蔡邕曰：「言天體者有三家：一曰周髀，二曰宣夜，三曰渾天。

translations.literal: Memorials of Han Famous Ministers record Cai Yong as saying: "Those who speak of the celestial body follow three schools: first the Zhou bi, second the Xuan ye, third the Hun tian."
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong, in the collection of ministerial memorials, names three cosmological schools: Zhou bi, Xuan ye, and Hun tian.

translations.literal: The learning of Xuan ye is cut off; there is no transmitted method.
translations.idiomatic: The Xuan ye teaching died out and left no living transmission.

sentences.zh: 周髀術數具存，考驗天狀，多所違失，故史官不用。

translations.literal: The Zhou bi numerology survives complete, but when tested against the sky's shape it often misses, so the historiographers do not use it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou bi math survives intact yet fails observational checks, which is why the court astronomers set it aside.

sentences.zh: 唯渾天者，近得其情，今史官所用候台銅儀，則其法也。

translations.literal: Only the Hun tian comes near the truth; the bronze instruments on the observatory tower that the historiographers use today follow that method.
translations.idiomatic: Only the armillary Hun tian model fits the sky; the bronze instruments on the imperial observatory follow that tradition.

sentences.zh: 」靈憲序曰：「昔在先王，將步天路，用定靈軌。

translations.literal: " The Lingxian preface says: "Of old the former kings, about to pace the road of heaven, used it to fix the spirit tracks."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng's Lingxian preface begins: the ancient kings, before mapping heaven's road, first fixed the spirit tracks.

sentences.zh: 尋緒本元，先准之於渾體，是為正儀，故靈憲作興。

translations.literal: Tracing the thread to the root origin, first aligning it with the armillary body—this is the correct instrument; therefore the Lingxian arose.
translations.idiomatic: They traced principle to its root and aligned it with the armillary sphere as the true gauge—hence the Lingxian.

sentences.zh: 」衡集無筭罔論，蓋網絡天地而筭之，因名焉。

translations.literal: " In Heng's collected works there is no separate treatise called Suanshu Lun; probably it netted heaven and earth to calculate them, and took its name from that.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng's collected works lack a separate Suanshu Lun; the title likely referred to a computational net cast over heaven and earth.

translations.literal: Xian here means "wrong."
translations.idiomatic: Here xian means "idle" or "off the mark," not "leisure."

sentences.zh: 衡集云：「觀者，觀余去史官五載而復還，非進取之勢也。

translations.literal: Heng's collected works say: "The 'observer' observes that I left the historian's office for five years and then returned—not a posture of advancing."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng explains the word "observer": someone watching him leave the astrologer's post for five years and return—not a careerist maneuver.

sentences.zh: 唯衡內識利鈍，操心不改。

translations.literal: Only Heng inwardly knows sharp and dull, and his heart does not change.
translations.idiomatic: Only Zhang Heng, knowing his own strengths and limits, kept his purpose unchanged.

sentences.zh: 或不我知者，以為失志矣。

translations.literal: Those who do not understand me suppose I have lost my ambition.
translations.idiomatic: Those who misunderstood him thought he had abandoned his aims.

translations.literal: Therefore it is used as "idle critic."
translations.idiomatic: Hence the piece takes the title Response to Idle Criticism.

sentences.zh: 余應之以時有遇否，性命難求，因茲以露余誠焉，名之應閒厔。」

translations.literal: I answer him that fortune and obstruction come in turn, life and fate are hard to seek; therefore I reveal my sincerity in this, and name it Response to Idle Criticism."
translations.idiomatic: He answers that success and setback alternate and fate cannot be forced; he lays out his sincerity under the title Response to Idle Criticism.

translations.literal: The Analects records Confucius as saying: "I study what is below and penetrate what is above."
translations.idiomatic: The Analects quotes Confucius: study the human realm below to comprehend what lies above.

translations.literal: " The commentary says: "Study human affairs below, know Heaven's mandate above."
translations.idiomatic: The gloss reads: learn the human world below, grasp Heaven's mandate above.

translations.literal: The Documents says: "Establish merit and establish affairs, and you may prolong the years."
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu promises long life to those who build lasting deeds.

sentences.zh: 」逸詩曰：「祈招之愔愔，式昭德音。

translations.literal: " A lost ode says: "Prayer's summons is gentle and still; thereby display virtuous sound."
translations.idiomatic: A lost ode from the tradition speaks of gentle prayer and a voice that proclaims virtue.

translations.literal: " Shi means "use."
translations.idiomatic: Shi here means "to employ" or "thereby."

translations.literal: Zhao means "make bright."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao means to illuminate or make manifest.

sentences.zh: 《尚書》伊尹曰：「予弗克俾厥後，惟堯舜其心，愧恥若撻於巿。

translations.literal: The Documents records Yi Yin as saying: "If I cannot make my successor's heart that of Yao and Shun, I feel shame as though whipped in the marketplace."
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu has Yi Yin swear to instill Yao and Shun's heart in his prince or feel market-square shame.

translations.literal: " Jing means "make manifest."
translations.idiomatic: Jing means to display or make plain.

translations.literal: Su means "intent" here.
translations.idiomatic: Su here carries the sense of inner purpose or plain substance.

translations.literal: Gao Dan and Wu Xian were both worthy ministers of Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Dan and Wu Xun served as eminent ministers of the Shang house.

translations.literal: The Documents says: "Gao Dan dwelt in clarity."
translations.idiomatic: The Shang shu says Gao Dan made his dwelling in enlightened rule.

translations.literal: " It also says "Wu Xian preserved and ordered the royal house."
translations.idiomatic: The same canon adds that Wu Xun guarded and ordered the royal house.

translations.literal: The Earl of Shen was the lord of Shen state;
translations.idiomatic: The Earl of Shen ruled the state of Shen;

sentences.zh: 樊仲，仲山甫也，為樊侯：並周宣王之卿士。

translations.literal: Fan Zhong is Zhongshanfu, who became Marquis of Fan: both were ministers to King Xuan of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zhong is Zhong Shanfu, enfeoffed as marquis of Fan—both were senior ministers to King Xuan of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 《詩·大雅》曰：「維申及甫，維周之翰。

translations.literal: The Odes, Greater Ya, says: "Shen and Fu are the bulwarks of Zhou."
translations.idiomatic: The Greater Ya sings that Shen and Fu are Zhou's protecting ramparts.

translations.literal: " The gloss: "Han means shield."
translations.idiomatic: The commentary glosses han as shield or protector.

sentences.zh: 服袞謂申伯為頤宰，服袞冕之服也。

translations.literal: Wearing ceremonial robes means the Earl of Shen served as chief cook and wore the robes and crown of that office.
translations.idiomatic: "Robed for court" means the Earl of Shen acted as chief steward in full ritual dress.

translations.literal: " It also says: "I grant you a great jade tablet to be your treasure."
translations.idiomatic: The ode continues: the king grants a tall jade sceptre as his token.

translations.literal: " The commentary says: "Bao means auspicious token."
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains bao as a lucky emblem.

translations.literal: A tablet one chi two cun long is called jie."
translations.idiomatic: A sceptre measuring one chi two cun is called the "great" jade.

translations.literal: The Changes, Appended Remarks, says "Supreme virtue and the great enterprise—how they reach the utmost!
translations.idiomatic: The Ten Wings declare that consummate virtue and the great enterprise touch the ultimate.

sentences.zh: 富有之謂大業，日新之謂盛德」也。

translations.literal: To be rich and complete is called the great enterprise; to renew daily is called supreme virtue."
translations.idiomatic: Completeness is named the great enterprise; daily renewal is named supreme virtue.

translations.literal: The Analects says: "Be steadfast in faith and fond of learning.
translations.idiomatic: The Analects urges firm faith and love of study.

translations.literal: " It also says: "The humane man rests in humaneness.
translations.idiomatic: It adds that the truly humane find their rest in humaneness alone.

translations.literal: " It also says: "The more you bore into it, the harder it becomes.
translations.idiomatic: Another line says the Way grows harder the deeper you drill.

translations.literal: " "He broadens me with culture and restrains me with ritual."
translations.idiomatic: And: "He widens me with letters and reins me with rite."

translations.literal: Riguan is an astrologer-official.
translations.idiomatic: Riguan means the court astronomer.

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: "The Son of Heaven has a sun-official."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo speaks of the sun-officer at the Zhou court.

translations.literal: " The Erya says: "Yuan means again."
translations.idiomatic: The Erya glosses yuan as "once more."

translations.literal: Laozi says: "Bending, then whole; crooked, then—*
translations.idiomatic: Laozi teaches that yielding preserves wholeness; bent, then—

translations.literal: (Gloss: ) (See Chinese subcommentary for full quotation.)
translations.idiomatic: The critical apparatus marks ().

translations.literal: Collation: *［］。
translations.idiomatic: Collation entry: *［］。

translations.literal: " He also says: "The level Way seems rough; advancing in the Way seems like retreating."
translations.idiomatic: Laozi adds that the smooth path looks jagged and forward motion can feel like falling back.

translations.literal: " The Yi's "Miscellaneous Hexagrams" says: "Need means not advancing."
translations.idiomatic: The Miscellany on the Changes defines Need as refusal to rush ahead.

translations.literal: Heaven's thoroughfare means Heaven's Way.
translations.idiomatic: Tianqu names the high road of Heaven's pattern.

sentences.zh: 言徒銳思作靈憲、渾天儀等也。

translations.literal: This means he only sharpened his thought to compose the Lingxian, armillary sphere, and the like.
translations.idiomatic: The line notes he poured wit into the Lingxian and bronze instruments rather than into politics.

translations.literal: Jie means to hike the robe; pronunciation qiu-li fan.
translations.idiomatic: Jie is to gather the skirt wading deep; read quli.

translations.literal: The Odes, Bei Airs, says: "Where it is deep, wade; where shallow, lift the hem."
translations.idiomatic: The Bei ode tells us to ford deep water and hike the robe in the shallows.

sentences.zh: 」爾雅曰：「由帶以上為厲，由膝以下為揭。

translations.literal: " The Erya says: "From the girdle upward is wading; from the knee downward is lifting the hem."
translations.idiomatic: The Erya defines the two depths for crossing streams.

sentences.zh: 」言遭時制宜，遇深水則厲，淺則揭也。

translations.literal: " It means suiting action to the hour: meeting deep water, wade; shallow, lift the hem.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: adapt the crossing to depth—wade or hike as the ford demands.

translations.literal: The Yi on the hexagram Sui: "How great is the meaning of suiting the time!"
translations.idiomatic: The Sui hexagram praises timeliness above all.

sentences.zh: 」《莊子》曰：「朱泙曼學屠龍於支離益，單千金之家，三年技成而無所用。

translations.literal: " The Zhuangzi says: "Zhu Pingman studied dragon-slaughter under Zhili Yi, exhausted a thousand in gold, and after three years had a skill with no use."
translations.idiomatic: Zhuangzi's Zhu Pingman spent a fortune learning a useless art from Zhili Yi.

translations.literal: Ji read qu-qi fan.
translations.idiomatic: Skill is read in the rising tone (quqi).

sentences.zh: 責衡何獨妙思於機巧者也。

translations.literal: It blames Heng: why should he alone pour subtle thought into ingenious devices?
translations.idiomatic: The critic asks why Zhang Heng alone lavishes wit on machines.

translations.literal: San read san (three).
translations.idiomatic: San is the numeral three.

sentences.zh: 垂翅故棲，謂再為史官也。

translations.literal: "Folding wings to the old roost" means serving again as historian.
translations.idiomatic: The folded wings line means returning to the historian's post.

translations.literal: He means "why not."
translations.idiomatic: He is the classical "why not."

translations.literal: Xian means sharp.
translations.idiomatic: Xian means keen or sharpened.

translations.literal: Zhu is the particle zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu equals the genitive particle.

sentences.zh: 閒者言衡作三輪木雕，尚能飛轉，已乃垂翅故棲，何不調其機關使利而高飛邪？

translations.literal: The critic asks: Heng made a three-wheeled wooden carving that could still fly and spin—then he folds his wings to his old nest; why not tune the mechanism sharp and soar high?
translations.idiomatic: The question: if a wooden bird could fly, why not tune the gears and rise instead of settling?

sentences.zh: 《傅子》曰「張衡能令三輪獨轉」也。

translations.literal: The Fu zi says "Zhang Heng could make three wheels turn by themselves."
translations.idiomatic: The Fu zi credits Zhang Heng with self-turning triple wheels.

sentences.zh: 《詩·大雅·文王篇》曰「永言配命，自求多福」也。

translations.literal: The Odes, Greater Ya, "King Wen," says "Long match your mandate, seeking much blessing for yourself."
translations.idiomatic: King Wen's ode bids us align with Heaven's charge and win our own blessings.

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: "Human life rests in diligence; with diligence there is no want."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo says life turns on toil; the diligent never lack.

translations.literal: " It also says: "If you do not seek, what will you gain? I mean to seek it."
translations.idiomatic: It adds: without striving you harvest nothing—and the speaker means to strive.

translations.literal: Ke means overcome.
translations.idiomatic: Ke means to overcome or best.

translations.literal: Heng's collected works read "fine words to buy favor."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Heng's own text has "fine words to buy favor."

sentences.zh: 《詩·小雅》曰：「伐木丁丁，鳥鳴嚶嚶，出自幽谷，遷於喬木。

translations.literal: The Odes, Lesser Ya, says: "Woodcutters' ding-ding, birds' crying ying-ying; out of the deep valley, up to the tall tree."
translations.idiomatic: The Lesser Ya images birds leaving the dell for the high bough.

sentences.zh: 」喻求仕遷於高位，振揚德音，如金玉之聲。

translations.literal: " It likens seeking office and moving to high rank to raising a virtuous tone like metal and jade.
translations.idiomatic: The image is promotion that rings like bronze and jade.

translations.literal: Mencius says: "Metal sound and jade vibration [for it]."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius compares true learning to the strike of metal answered by jade.

translations.literal: Lin means shame.
translations.idiomatic: Lin is shame or stinginess of honor.

translations.literal: The Zuo Tradition says: "Duke Song of Song jeered at him."
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo uses jin for public ridicule.

translations.literal: " Du Yu's note: "Teasing so as to shame one another is called jin."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu defines jin as playful shaming.

sentences.zh: 《方言》曰：「凡物盛而多，齊宋之郊謂之伙。

translations.literal: The Fangyan says: "Where things abound, in the Qi-Song border they call it huo."
translations.idiomatic: The Fangyan records huo for abundance near Qi and Song.

translations.literal: Read he-guo fan.
translations.idiomatic: Pronunciation heguo.

sentences.zh: 《孟子》曰：「仁義忠信，樂善不倦，此天爵也。

translations.literal: Mencius says: "Humaneness, right, loyalty, and trust, delighting in the good without weariness—these are Heaven's nobility."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius lists Heaven's innate honors: humaneness, right, loyalty, tireless love of good.

translations.literal: Dukes, ministers, and great officers are the nobility of man.
translations.idiomatic: Court rank is the nobility that men confer.

sentences.zh: 」案：此謂天子高縣爵位，得者在命也。

translations.literal: Case: this means the Son of Heaven suspends high rank; obtaining it lies in fate.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: the throne dangles high rank, but fate decides who wins it.

translations.literal: Su means summon.
translations.idiomatic: Su means to summon or call.

translations.literal: Huai means come.
translations.idiomatic: Huai here means "come to you."

translations.literal: Zhan is the particle zhi.
translations.idiomatic: Zhan equals the particle zhi.

translations.literal: Mian means turn the back on.
translations.idiomatic: Mian is to turn away or ignore.

translations.literal: Dian means peril.
translations.idiomatic: Dian means to stand on the brink of danger.

sentences.zh: 《孟子》陳代問孟子曰：「枉尺而直尋，若可為也？

translations.literal: Mencius: Chen Dai asked Mencius, "Bend a foot to straighten eight feet—may one do it?"
translations.idiomatic: Chen Dai asks whether crooking an inch to gain a yard is allowable.

sentences.zh: 」孟子曰：「昔齊景公田，招虞人以旌，不到，將殺之。

translations.literal: " Mencius said: "Once Duke Jing of Qi hunted and waved a banner to summon the park-keeper; the man would not come, and the duke meant to kill him."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius recalls Duke Jing's unjust summons of the warden.

sentences.zh: 志士不忘在溝壑，如不待招而往，何哉？

translations.literal: " A resolute man does not forget he may end in ditch or gully; how could he go unbidden—what then?"
translations.idiomatic: The worthy remembers he may die in a ditch; he will not answer a wrongful summons.

sentences.zh: 且夫枉尺而直尋者，以利言也。

translations.literal: " Moreover to bend a foot and straighten eight feet speaks of profit."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius says the inch-for-yard argument is really about gain.

sentences.zh: 如以利，則枉尋直尺而利，亦可為歟？

translations.literal: " If we speak of profit, then bend eight feet to straighten a foot for profit—may that be done?"
translations.idiomatic: If profit rules, why not sacrifice a yard for an inch?

translations.literal: " Zhao Qi's commentary: "Resolute man means one who guards right."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi defines the resolute man as the guardian of duty.

sentences.zh: 君子固窮，故虞人不得其招尚不往，如何君子不*

translations.literal: The nobleman is secure in want, so the park-keeper would not go without the proper summons—how could the nobleman not*
translations.idiomatic: The gentleman endures poverty; the warden refused a bad summons—how should the gentleman

translations.literal: (Gloss: ) (See Chinese subcommentary for full quotation.)
translations.idiomatic: The critical apparatus marks ().

translations.literal: Collation: *［］。
translations.idiomatic: Collation entry: *［］。

sentences.zh: 尺小尋大，不可枉大就小，而以要利也。」

translations.literal: inch small, foot large—one must not bend the large to the small to seek profit."
translations.idiomatic: trade a large span for a tiny crook for the sake of profit?"

translations.literal: Cai means suspicion.
translations.idiomatic: Cai is suspicion or distrust.

translations.literal: Gui is a food vessel.
translations.idiomatic: A gui is a grain bowl.

translations.literal: Sun read sun.
translations.idiomatic: Sun is read in the level tone.

translations.literal: The Odes says: "There is a covered basket of cooked grain."
translations.idiomatic: The Odes speaks of a covered basket of food.

sentences.zh: 」饌音仕卷反，餔音補故反，並謂食也。

translations.literal: " Zhuan read shi-juan fan; bu read bu-gu fan; both mean food.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan and bu are both glossed as food, with fanqie readings given.

translations.literal: Xie means "scruple" here.
translations.idiomatic: Xie here means scruple or refuse.

translations.literal: Yi means use.
translations.idiomatic: Yi means "to use."

translations.literal: Yuan Mingmao was a starving man.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Mingmao is the starving worthy of legend.

translations.literal: One text writes "Yuan Jingmu."
translations.idiomatic: An alternate text reads Yuan Jingmu.

sentences.zh: 《列子》曰：「東方有人焉，曰爰旌目，將有適也，而餓於道。

translations.literal: The Liezi says: "In the east was a man called Yuan Jingmu; about to travel, he starved by the road."
translations.idiomatic: Liezi tells of Yuan Jingmu starving on the road.

sentences.zh: 狐丘父之盜曰丘，見而下壺飧以餔之。

translations.literal: A robber of Huqiu's father, named Qiu, saw him and set down a kettle of food to feed him.
translations.idiomatic: A bandit named Qiu from Huqiu offered him food.

sentences.zh: 爰旌目三餔而後能視，曰：『子何為者？

translations.literal: Yuan Jingmu ate three times before he could see, and said, "What manner of man are you?"
translations.idiomatic: After three meals his sight returned and he asked who fed him.
The lacuna marks a break in the quoted dialogue.

translations.literal: (Gloss: ) (See Chinese subcommentary for full quotation.)
translations.idiomatic: The critical apparatus marks ().

translations.literal: Collation: *［］：『。
translations.idiomatic: Collation entry: *［］：『。

translations.literal: ' Yuan Jingmu said, "Ha—you are not a robber, are you?"
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Jingmu then cries, "You must be a robber!"

translations.literal: " I will not eat your food on principle."
translations.idiomatic: He refuses the food on grounds of honor.

sentences.zh: 』兩手據地而歐之，不出，喀喀而死。」

translations.literal: " He pressed both hands to the earth and vomited; it would not come out, and he died choking."
translations.idiomatic: He tries to vomit the meal and chokes to death.

sentences.zh: 《孟子》：「陳臻問曰：『前於齊，王饋兼金一百而不受；

translations.literal: The Mencius: "Chen Zhen asked, 'Earlier in Qi the king sent a hundred yi of doubled gold and you would not take it;
translations.idiomatic: Chen Zhen recalls Mencius refusing a hundred yi in Qi.

translations.literal: in Song they sent seventy yi and you took it."
translations.idiomatic: In Song a smaller gift was accepted.

sentences.zh: 前日之不受是，則今受之非也？

translations.literal: If not taking it earlier was right, is taking it now wrong?"
translations.idiomatic: He asks whether consistency forbids taking the second gift.

translations.literal: ' Mencius said, "Both were right."
translations.idiomatic: Mencius answers that both choices were right.

sentences.zh: 當在宋也，予將遠行，遠行者必以贐，予何為不受？

translations.literal: " In Song I was about to travel far; travelers must receive travel funds—why should I not take it?"
translations.idiomatic: In Song he needed travel money for a long journey.

sentences.zh: 若於齊，則未有處也，無處而饋之，是貨之也。

translations.literal: " In Qi there was yet no occasion; to give with no occasion is to bribe."
translations.idiomatic: In Qi there was no proper reason, so the gold would have been a bribe.

sentences.zh: 焉有君子而可以貨取乎？

translations.literal: " How could a gentleman be bought with goods?"
translations.idiomatic: No gentleman sells himself for gold.

translations.literal: '" Zhao Qi's commentary: "Doubled gold means fine gold."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qi explains doubled gold as refined metal.

sentences.zh: 價兼倍於惡者，故曰兼金。

translations.literal: Its price doubles the poor sort, hence "doubled gold."
translations.idiomatic: It costs twice ordinary gold, hence the name.

translations.literal: One hundred means one hundred yi.
translations.idiomatic: One hundred counts one hundred yi ingots.

translations.literal: Twenty liang make one yi.
translations.idiomatic: Twenty liang equal one yi.

translations.literal: Jin is the traveler's parting gift.
translations.idiomatic: Jin is the farewell gift for the road.

sentences.zh: 在齊時無事，於義未有所處也。

translations.literal: In Qi there was no affair; in right there was no occasion.
translations.idiomatic: In Qi he had no official business, hence no moral occasion to accept.

sentences.zh: 義無所處而饋之，是以貨賄*

translations.literal: With no rightful occasion yet giving it, that is bribery with goods*
translations.idiomatic: Gifts without moral ground are plain bribery

translations.literal: Collation: *，。」
translations.idiomatic: Collation entry: *，。」

translations.literal: Stripping the hemp jacket means Ning Qi.
translations.idiomatic: "Stripping coarse cloth" alludes to Ning Qi.

translations.literal: Laying aside shovel and trowel means Fu Yue.
translations.idiomatic: "Laying down builder's tools" alludes to Fu Yue.

translations.literal: Shu read chang-zhu fan.
translations.idiomatic: Shu is read changzhu.

sentences.zh: 方言曰「自關而西，謂襜褕短者謂之裋」也。

translations.literal: The Fangyan says: "West of the passes they call a short unlined coat shu."
translations.idiomatic: Fangyan defines shu as a short coat west of the passes.

translations.literal: Some manuscripts write "yuan" for "shou."
translations.idiomatic: Some editions write yuan instead of shou.

translations.literal: Tongmeng means not yet clear.
translations.idiomatic: Tongmeng means dim-sighted, not yet enlightened.

sentences.zh: 《史記》曰：「黃帝迎日推策，舉風後、力牧以理人，順天地之紀，幽明之占。

sentences.zh: 」春秋內事曰：「黃帝師於風後，風後善於伏羲氏之道，故推演陰陽之事。

sentences.zh: 」蓺文志陰陽流有風後十三篇也。

sentences.zh: 冬至日南極而影長，夜六十刻，晝四十刻也。

translations.literal: The Shuowen says: "Dragon, chief of scaly creatures, can be hidden or bright, small or large, short or long; at the vernal equinox it mounts heaven, at the autumn equinox it enters the stream."
